blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا (١)
Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, (1)
لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنۢبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٢)
that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may perfect His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path, (2)
وَيَنصُرَكَ ٱللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا (٣)
and Allah will help you with a mighty help. (3)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا مَّعَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٤)
It is He who sent down composure into the hearts of the faithful that they might enhance in their faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (4)
لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَيُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٥)
That He may admit the faithful, men and women, into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that He may absolve them of their misdeeds. That is a great success with Allah. (5)
وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ ٱلظَّآنِّينَ بِٱللَّهِ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَنَهُمْ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (٦)
That He may punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women, who entertain a bad opinion of Allah. For them shall be an adverse turn of fortune: Allah is wrathful with them and He has cursed them, and prepared hell for them and it is an evil destination. (6)
وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (٧)
To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (7)

(Commentary)

The contents of the verses of this Surah, in their various sections, clearly apply to the story of the Treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah (صلح الحديبية), which took place in the sixth year of the Hijrah (الهجرة), and the events that occurred around it, such as the story of the desert Arabs (الأعراب) who stayed behind, the obstruction by the polytheists (المشركين), and the Pledge of the Tree (بيعة الشجرة), as detailed in the historical reports (الآثار). A portion of these will be mentioned in the following "Discussion on Traditions," Allah the Exalted willing.

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the favor Allah the Exalted bestowed upon His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through the manifest victory (الفتح المبين) He granted him during this journey, and upon the believers who were with him. It contains profound praise for them and a beautiful promise for those among them who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات). The Surah is Medinan (مدنية).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً — "Indeed We have inaugurated for you a manifest victory" (48:1). This speech is situated in the context of bestowing a favor (الامتنان). The emphasis of the sentence with inna (إِنَّ), the attribution of the victory to the "We" of majesty (نون العظمة), and its description as "manifest" (المبين) are all intended to highlight the significance of the victory being bestowed as a favor.

What is meant by this victory, as supported by the contextual clues (قرائن الكلام), is the victory Allah granted His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the Treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah.

This is because what follows in the verses of the Surah—the bestowal of favor upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, the praise for them, the expression of pleasure (الرضا) regarding their pledge, the beautiful promise to them in this world of immediate and future spoils and in the Hereafter of Paradise, the censure of the desert Arabs who stayed behind when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called them to mobilize and they did not go out with him, the censure of the polytheists for obstructing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those with him, the censure of the hypocrites (المنافقين), Allah’s confirmation of His Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) vision, and His saying: فَعَلِمَ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا فَجَعَلَ مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً — "He knew what you did not know, and He appointed besides that a victory near at hand" (48:27), which is almost explicit—all these are meanings linked to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departure to Mecca for the pilgrimage and its conclusion in the Treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah.

As for this treaty being a "manifest victory" (فتحاً مبيناً) granted by Allah to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it becomes clear by contemplating the tone of the Surah’s verses regarding this story. The departure of the Prophet and the believers for this purpose was a departure fraught with great danger, such that their return to Medina would not normally be expected, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْقَلِبَ الرَّسُولُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلى أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَداً — "Rather you thought that the Apostle and the believers would never return to their families" (48:12). The polytheists, consisting of the chieftains of Quraysh and their followers, with all their prestige, power, and enmity toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, had for years allowed nothing between them but the sword; nothing had brought them together except the battlefield, such as the battles of Badr, Uhud, and al-Ahzab. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had gone out with only a small band—one thousand four hundred—who were of no consequence to the gathered forces of the polytheists in their own territory.

However, Allah, the Glorified, turned the matter in favor of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers against the polytheists. They agreed to what was neither hoped for nor expected from them; they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to make peace with them on the condition of ceasing hostilities for ten years, ensuring the safety of each party’s followers and those who joined them, and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would return to Medina that year and then come to Mecca the following year, where they would vacate the Mosque and the Kaaba for him for three days.

This was among the clearest of victories Allah granted His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It was one of the most direct causes for the Conquest of Mecca (فتح مكة) in the eighth year of the Hijrah. A great number of polytheists embraced Islam in the two years between the treaty and the conquest. Furthermore, Khaybar and its surrounding areas were conquered in the beginning of the seventh year, through which the Muslims grew strong, Islam expanded significantly, their numbers increased, their reputation spread, and they occupied many lands. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) set out for the Conquest of Mecca with ten thousand or twelve thousand men, whereas he had set out for Hudaybiyyah with only one thousand four hundred, as detailed in the historical reports.

It has been said that the victory refers to the Conquest of Mecca, so the meaning of His saying: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ (Indeed We have inaugurated for you) would be: "We have decreed for you the Conquest of Mecca." However, the contextual clues do not support this.

It has also been said that it refers to the conquest of Khaybar, meaning—assuming the Surah was revealed during the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return from Hudaybiyyah to Medina—"We have decreed for you the conquest of Khaybar." This view is like the previous one.

It has also been said that it refers to a spiritual victory (الفتح المعنوي), which is the triumph over enemies through clear proofs (الحجج البينة) and dazzling miracles (المعجزات الباهرة) by which the word of truth prevailed over falsehood and Islam became manifest over all religions. While this view is not inherently problematic, the context (سياق) of the verses does not harmonize with it.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللهُ ما تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَما تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً وَيَنْصُرَكَ اللهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً — "that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may perfect His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path, and that Allah may help you with a mighty help" (48:2-3). The lam (ل) in His saying لِيَغْفِرَ (that He may forgive) is for causation (التعليل), as is apparent from the wording. Thus, its literal meaning is that the purpose of this manifest victory is the forgiveness of what is past of your sin and what is to come. It is well known that there is no inherent link between victory and the forgiveness of sins, nor is there any rational meaning for attributing victory to forgiveness in this way.

The statement of some who, fleeing from this problem, said that the lam with a kasrah in لِيَغْفِرَ is the lam of an oath (لام القسم), and the original form was layaghfiranna (ليغفرن), where the nun of emphasis was deleted and the preceding letter remained with a fathah to indicate the deleted part—this is an error with no precedent in usage.

Similarly, the statement of others who, also fleeing from the problem, said: "The cause is the totality of forgiveness and what is coordinated with it, such as the perfection of blessing, guidance, and mighty help; thus, the fact that one part—namely the forgiveness of sin—is not a cause for victory in itself does not contradict the whole being a cause." This is a weak (سخيف) argument that yields no benefit. For the forgiveness of sin is neither a cause nor a part of a cause for victory, nor is it related in any way to what is coordinated with it such that its inclusion among the causes could be justified. There is no justification for mentioning it alone, nor along with the other causes.

In short, this problem is an excellent proof that "sin" (الذنب) in the verse does not refer to the well-known meaning of sin—which is the violation of a master’s command (مخالفة التكليف المولوي)—nor does "forgiveness" (المغفرة) refer to its well-known meaning—which is the waiving of punishment for said violation. Linguistically, "sin" (الذنب), as derived from its various usages, refers to an act that has an evil consequence (تبعة سيئة), whatever it may be. "Forgiveness" (المغفرة) is the covering (الستر) over something. As for the two meanings that immediately come to our minds today—namely, the violation of a master’s command followed by punishment and the waiving of that punishment—these are meanings attached to them according to the convention of the religious community (عرف المتشرعين).

The Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) undertaking of the mission (الدعوة) and his uprising against disbelief and idolatry before the Hijrah, his persistence in that, and the wars and military expeditions (المغازي) that occurred between him and the disbelievers and polytheists after the Hijrah, were all actions that had evil consequences (تبعة سيئة) in the eyes of the disbelievers and polytheists. They were not going to "forgive" him for that as long as they possessed power and capability. They were not going to forget the perishing of their creed and the destruction of their tradition and way of life, nor the blood-vengeance for their slain chieftains, without quenching the rage of their hearts by taking revenge on him, erasing his name, and obliterating his legacy. However, Allah, the Glorified, granted him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) this victory—whether it be the Conquest of Mecca or the Treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah leading to it—thereby removing their power and extinguishing their fire. Thus, He covered (ستر) for him what they considered a "sin" against them and secured him from them.

Therefore, "sin" (الذنب)—and Allah knows best—refers to the evil consequence that his mission had in the eyes of the disbelievers and polytheists; it was a "sin" they held against him, as in the saying of Moses to his Lord: وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنْبٌ فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ — "They have a [charge of] sin against me, and I fear they will kill me" (26:14). What was "past" of his sin was what occurred from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Mecca before the Hijrah, and what was "to come" was what occurred after the Hijrah. His (the Exalted) forgiveness of his sin was His covering it by nullifying its consequence through the removal of their power and the destruction of their structure. This is supported by what follows it: وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ (and that He may perfect His blessing upon you) until He says: وَيَنْصُرَكَ اللهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً (and that Allah may help you with a mighty help).

The commentators have other various views regarding the verse:

Among them: that "his sin" (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) refers to acts of disobedience (المعصية) committed by him, and "what is past and what is to come" refers to what occurred before his prophethood and after it. It has also been said: what occurred before the victory and what occurred after it.

This is flawed because it is based on the possibility of prophets (عليه‌السلام) committing sins, which contradicts what the Book, the Sunnah, and reason decisively establish regarding their infallibility (العصمة) (عليه‌السلام). This discussion has already been presented in the second volume of this book and elsewhere.

Furthermore, the problem of the lack of connection between the victory and forgiveness remains as it was.

Among them: that the forgiveness of what is past and what is to come refers to the forgiveness of sins that occurred and those that did not—meaning a promise to forgive future sins if they occur—so as to avoid the objection that forgiving a sin that has not happened is meaningless.

This is flawed, in addition to what was mentioned against the previous view, because forgiving future sins before they occur would imply the lifting of all religious obligations (التكاليف) from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entirely. This is refuted by the explicit text of His speech in many verses, such as: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ فَاعْبُدِ اللهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ — "Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth; so worship Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him" (39:2), and: وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "and I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit" (39:12), among other verses whose context refuses such specification.

Moreover, among sins and acts of disobedience are things like associating partners with Allah, fabricating lies against Allah, mocking the signs of Allah, spreading corruption on earth, and violating sanctities. The general term "forgiveness of sins" would include these. It makes no sense for Allah to send a servant, commanding him to establish His religion firmly and reform the earth, and then, when He grants him victory and makes him prevail as He wishes, to permit him to violate what He commanded, destroy what he built, and corrupt what he reformed, by forgiving every violation and sin from him and pardoning every fabrication he might invent against Allah. His actions in conveying the message are like his words, and Allah has said: وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنا بَعْضَ الْأَقاوِيلِ لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ — "Had he faked any sayings in Our name, We would have surely seized him by the right hand, then We would have surely cut off his aorta" (69:44-46).

Among them: the statement of some that the forgiveness of what is past of his sin refers to the forgiveness of the past sins of his parents, Adam and Eve (عليه السلام), through his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) blessing, and the forgiveness of what is to come refers to the forgiveness of the sins of his community (الأمة) through his supplication.

This is flawed for the same reasons mentioned against the previous views.

Among them: that the speech is in the sense of a hypothetical (التقدير) even though it is in the context of an actualization (التحقيق). The meaning would be: "That Allah may forgive you your old and new sins, were you to have any."

This is flawed because it adopts a meaning contrary to the literal sense without evidence.

Among them: that the statement is made in the manner of glorification and gracious address. The meaning is: "May Allah forgive you," as in His saying: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ — "May Allah excuse you! Why did you give them leave" (9:43).

This is flawed because the custom in this type of address is to use the form of a supplication (الدعاء), as has been said.

Among them: that "sin" in his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) case refers to "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى), which is the violation of advisory commands (الأوامر الإرشادية) rather than rebelling against the fulfillment of masterly obligations. Prophets, given their degrees of proximity (القرب), are held accountable for leaving what is better just as others are held accountable for well-known sins, as it is said: "The good deeds of the righteous are the bad deeds of those drawn near."

Among them: what a group of our companions (أصحابنا) accepted—that the forgiveness of what is past and what is to come refers to the forgiveness of the past and future sins of his community through his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intercession (الشفاعة). There is no harm in attributing the sins of his community to him due to the connection and causal link between him and his community.

This view and the one before it are safe from general objections, but the problem of the lack of connection between the victory and forgiveness remains.

Among them: what is narrated from Alam al-Huda (Sharif al-Murtada), may Allah have mercy on him, that "sin" (الذنب) is a verbal noun (المصدر), and a verbal noun can be attributed to both the subject and the object. Here, it is attributed to the object, meaning: "what they previously sinned against you by preventing you from Mecca and obstructing you from the Sacred Mosque." "Forgiveness" (المغفرة) in this sense would mean the removal and abrogation of the decrees of his enemies among the polytheists; i.e., Allah removes that from you and covers that blemish for you by the victory He grants you in Mecca, so you may enter it thereafter.

This view is close in approach to the one we first presented, and there is no harm in it were it not for some slight deviation from the literal sense of the verse.

In His saying: لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللهُ (that Allah may forgive you) etc., after His saying: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ (Indeed We have inaugurated for you), there is a transition (التفات) from the first person to the third person. Perhaps the reason for this is that the sum of the Surah is His (the Exalted) bestowal of favor upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers through the victory He granted, the sending down of tranquility (السكينة), help, and the rest of what He promised them therein. Thus, it was appropriate for the prevailing context in the Surah to be that of the third person, where He is mentioned by His name (Allah) and help is attributed to Him, as He is the one Whom His Prophet and the believers worship alone, in contrast to what the polytheists do not worship—for they only worship gods besides Him, hoping for their help, yet they do not help them.

As for the first-person plural context (التكلم مع الغير) which suggests majesty in the first verse, it is due to its appropriateness for mentioning the victory. The same applies to His saying to come: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً — "Indeed We have sent you as a witness" (48:8).

His saying: وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ (and that He may perfect His blessing upon you). It has been said: meaning He perfects it upon you in this world by making you prevail over your enemy, elevating your cause, and establishing your religion; and in the Hereafter by raising your rank. It has also been said: meaning He perfects it upon you through the conquests of Khaybar, Mecca, and Ta'if.

His saying: وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً (and guide you on a straight path). It has been said: meaning He keeps you firm on a path that leads its traveler to Paradise. It has also been said: meaning He guides you to the straightness of the path in conveying the laws and implementing the legal punishments (الحدود).

His saying: وَيَنْصُرَكَ اللهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً (and that Allah may help you with a mighty help). It has been said: "mighty help" (النصر العزيز) is that by which one is protected from every stubborn tyrant and rebellious transgressor. He indeed did that for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by making his religion the mightiest of religions and his authority the greatest of authorities. It has also been said: "mighty help" refers to that which is rare in existence, with few or no parallels. Allah’s help to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was such, as is evident by comparing his state at the beginning of his mission to his state in the final days of his call.

Contemplation of the context of the two verses, based on the previously mentioned meaning of His saying: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللهُ ما تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَما تَأَخَّرَ, suggests that the meaning of His saying: وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ is His preparing the way for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to complete the Word and clearing the atmosphere for his mighty help after removing the obstacles through the forgiveness of what was past of his sin and what was to come.

And by His saying: وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً, His guiding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—after clearing the atmosphere for him—to the path leading to the goal, which he traversed after returning from Hudaybiyyah, including the conquest of Khaybar and the expansion of the religion’s authority across the regions of the Peninsula until it culminated in the Conquest of Mecca and Ta'if.

And by His saying: وَيَنْصُرَكَ اللهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً, His helping him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with that manifest and dazzling help which is rarely—if ever—paralleled. For He opened for him Mecca and Ta'if, Islam spread throughout the land of the Peninsula, polytheism was uprooted, the Jews were humbled, and the Christians and Magians residing in the Peninsula submitted to him. Thus, He (the Exalted) perfected for the people their religion, completed His blessing upon them, and approved Islam as their religion.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ — "It is He who sent down composure into the hearts of the believers that they might enhance in their faith along with their [present] faith" (48:4) etc. It is apparent that "tranquility" (السكينة) refers to the stillness of the soul, its firmness, and its peace (الاطمئنان) regarding what it has believed in. Therefore, He attributed its sending down to the purpose: لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ (that they might enhance in their faith along with their [present] faith). The discussion on tranquility has already been presented in the commentary on His saying: أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ التَّابُوتُ فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "that the Ark should come to you, containing a composure from your Lord" (2:248) in the second volume of this book, where we mentioned that it corresponds to the "spirit of faith" (روح الإيمان) mentioned in His saying: وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "and He has strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (58:22).

It has been said: tranquility (السكينة) is mercy (الرحمة); it has been said: it is reason (العقل); it has been said: it is dignity (الوقار) and protection (العصمة) for Allah and His Messenger; it has been said: it is the inclination toward what the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought; it has been said: it is an angel that dwells in the heart of the believer; and it has been said: it is something with a head like that of a cat. There is no evidence for any of these claims.

The meaning of "sending down" (الإنزال) tranquility into their hearts is creating it within them after its absence. Often in the Quran, creation and origination (الإيجاد) are expressed as "sending down," such as His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ — "and He has sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6), and His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ — "and We sent down iron" (57:25), and His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but its treasures are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). Creation and origination are expressed as "sending down" to indicate the loftiness of its source.

It has been said: "sending down" (الإنزال) means settling and establishing, from the expression: "he descended (نزل) in such a place," meaning he unpacked his belongings there, and "I settled him (أنزلته) in it," meaning I made him unpack there.

This is a meaning not customary in His (the Exalted) speech despite its frequent occurrence therein. Perhaps what prompted them to choose this meaning was its being made transitive in the verse with the preposition "in" (في), as He said: أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوفِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (sent down composure into the hearts of the believers). However, this is a literary nuance (عناية كلامية) in which the connection of tranquility to the hearts is viewed as a connection of stability within them, just as its connection is viewed as a connection of falling upon them from above in His saying to come: فأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ (so He sent down composure upon them) and His saying: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the believers).

The meaning of "increase in faith" (زيادة الإيمان) is its intensification. For faith in something is knowledge of it accompanied by commitment (الالتزام) such that its practical effects follow. It is well known that both knowledge and the aforementioned commitment are subject to intensification and weakening; thus, faith—which is knowledge clothed in commitment—intensifies and weakens.

So the meaning of the verse is: Allah is He Who originated the firmness and peace (الاطمئنان) which is a necessary consequence of one of the levels of the spirit in the hearts of the believers, so that the faith they possessed before the descent of tranquility might intensify, becoming more perfect than it was before.

A Discourse on Faith and its Increase

Faith (الإيمان) in something is not merely the knowledge (العلم) attained regarding it, as is inferred from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ ارْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَى — "Indeed those who turned back after guidance had become manifest to them" (47:25), and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدى — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become manifest to them" (47:32), and His saying: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts" (24:14), and His saying: وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ — "and Allah has led him astray despite [his] knowledge" (45:23). These verses—as you see—establish apostasy, disbelief, denial, and misguidance despite the presence of knowledge.

Thus, mere knowledge of something and the certainty that it is true is not sufficient for the attainment of faith or for the one who possesses it to be described by it. Rather, there must be a commitment (الالتزام) to its implications and a binding of the heart to its content such that its practical effects follow, even if only partially. He who attains knowledge that Allah, the Exalted, is the God besides Whom there is no other, and commits to its implication—which is his servitude (العبودية) and his worship of Him alone—is a believer. If he knows it but does not commit, and thus performs none of the acts that manifest servitude, he is a knower (عالم) but not a believer (مؤمن).

From this, the falsity of the claim that faith is mere knowledge and assent (التصديق) becomes apparent, because, as mentioned, knowledge may coexist with disbelief.

From this also, the falsity of the claim that faith is action (العمل) becomes apparent, because action may coexist with hypocrisy (النفاق). A hypocrite performs actions, and he may even be one to whom the truth has appeared with scientific clarity, yet he has no faith in any case.

Since faith is knowledge of something accompanied by commitment to it such that its practical effects follow, and since both knowledge and commitment are subject to increase and decrease, and intensification and weakening, then faith—composed of the two—is capable of increase and decrease, and intensification and weakening. The variation of levels and the disparity of degrees are among the necessities about which there is no doubt whatsoever.

This is the view held by the majority, and it is the truth. It is supported by the transmitted evidence of His saying, the Exalted: لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ (that they might enhance in their faith along with their [present] faith) and other verses, as well as the traditions (أحاديث) narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) indicating that faith possesses levels.

A group, including Abu Hanifa, Imam al-Haramayn, and others, held that faith neither increases nor decreases. They argued that faith is a name for the assent (التصديق) that reaches the level of certainty (الجزم) and decisiveness (القطع), and these do not admit of increase or decrease. Thus, if a person who assents adds acts of obedience to his assent, or adds acts of disobedience, his assent remains as it is, not changing at all.

They interpreted the verses indicating its capacity for increase and decrease by saying that faith is an accident (عرض) that does not persist in its specific identity but rather through the renewal of likes (تجدد الأمثال). Thus, in terms of its application over time through its renewed likes, it increases and decreases—such as its occurring for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), for example, continuously without any intervening gap, while for others it occurs with few or many gaps. Thus, the meaning of the increase of faith is the succession of the parts of faith without any gap at all, or with few gaps.

Also, faith possesses multiplicity according to the multiplicity of what is believed in. Since the laws of the religion were revealed gradually and the believers believed in what was revealed of them, and the number of rulings increased time after time, their faith also increased gradually. In short, the meaning of the increase of faith is its numerical increase.

This is clearly weak. As for their argument, firstly: their claim that faith is a name for decisive assent is rejected; rather, it is a name for decisive assent accompanied by commitment, as previously explained—unless they mean by "assent" knowledge with commitment.

Secondly: their claim that this assent does not vary by increase and decrease is a claim without evidence; rather, it is a circular argument (مصادرة على المطلوب). Basing it on faith being an accident and the persistence of accidents being through the renewal of likes does not help them at all. For some faith is such that it is not moved by storms, while some vanishes at the slightest cause or the weakest doubt that arises. This cannot be explained by the renewal of likes or the scarcity or frequency of gaps; rather, it must be attributed to the strength and weakness of faith, whether we accept the renewal of likes or not.

Furthermore, the theory of the renewal of likes is false, as demonstrated in its proper place.

Their claim that if a person who assents adds acts of obedience or disobedience, his state does not change at all, is rejected. The strengthening of faith through the practice of obedience and its weakening through the commission of disobedience is something that should not be doubted. The strength and weakness of an effect (الأثر) reveal the strength and weakness of the source of the effect (مبدأ الأثر). The Exalted said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ — "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct" (35:10), and He said: ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ وَكانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — "Then the fate of those who committed evil was the worst [fate], for they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them" (30:10).

As for the interpretations they mentioned, the first of the two interpretations necessitates that one who has not perfected faith—who, according to them, has gaps in his heart devoid of the parts of faith—would be both a believer and a disbeliever in reality. This is something not supported nor even suggested by any of His (the Exalted) speech.

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ — "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners [to Him]" (12:106), it is closer to indicating that faith is something that increases and decreases than to indicating its denial. For its meaning is that they are believers while they are polytheists; thus, their faith is faith relative to pure polytheism, and polytheism relative to pure faith. This is the meaning of faith’s capacity for increase and decrease.

The second of the two interpretations implies that the increase in faith and its multiplicity is only due to the multiplicity of what it relates to—namely the rulings and laws revealed by Allah. Thus, it is a description of faith in terms of its object (متعلقه), and the cause of its being so described is its object. If this increase were what is meant by His saying: لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ, it would have been more appropriate to make the increase of faith in the verse the goal of the legislation and revelation of many rulings, not the goal of sending down tranquility into the hearts of the believers.

Some have interpreted the increase of faith in the verse as the increase of its effect (أثره), which is the light (النور) shining from it upon the heart.

This is flawed because the increase and strength of an effect is a branch of the increase and strength of the cause (المؤثر). It makes no sense to specify one of two things, equal in all respects, with an effect that exceeds the effect of the other.

Some mentioned that the faith which is the object of "along with" (مع) in His saying: لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ is the primordial faith (الإيمان الفطري), and the faith mentioned before it is the inferential faith (الإيمان الاستدلالي). The meaning would be: "that they might increase in inferential faith upon their primordial faith."

This is flawed as it is a claim without evidence. Furthermore, primordial faith is also inferential; the object of knowledge and faith is, in any case, a theoretical (نظري) matter, not a self-evident (بديهي) one.

Some, like Imam al-Razi, said: The dispute over whether faith accepts increase and decrease or not is a verbal dispute (نزاع لفظي). The intention of those who deny it is that the essence of faith (أصل الإيمان)—which is assent—does not accept it, and that is correct because it does not accept increase and decrease. The intention of those who affirm it is that what constitutes the perfection of faith—namely actions—accepts increase and decrease, and that is undoubtedly correct.

This is flawed, firstly: because it involves a confusion between assent and faith. Faith is assent with commitment, not just mere assent, as previously explained.

Secondly: attributing the denial of increase in the essence of faith to those who affirm it is incorrect. They indeed affirm the increase in the essence of faith, and they see that both knowledge and commitment, which compose faith, accept strength and weakness.

Thirdly: including actions in the subject of the dispute is incorrect, because a dispute over a thing is different from a dispute over its effect by which it is perfected. No one disputes that actions and acts of obedience accept being counted and being few or many according to the repetition of the unit.

His saying: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth" (48:4). The host (الجند) is a thick gathering of people when they are united by a purpose for which they act; hence it is applied to the soldiers (العسكر) gathered to carry out what their commander orders. The context testifies that what is meant by the hosts of the heavens and the earth are the causes (الأسباب) present in the world, both visible and invisible among creation. They are the mediating intermediaries between Him (the Exalted) and whatever thing He wills; they obey Him and do not disobey Him.

The mention of this sentence—namely His saying: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ etc.—after His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ etc., is to indicate that to Him belong all the causes and reasons present in existence. Thus, He may reach whatever He wills by whatever means He wills, and nothing can overcome Him in that. It has been linked to the increase of the believers’ faith through the sending down of tranquility into their hearts.

His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً — "and Allah is All-mighty, All-wise" (48:4). Meaning: His side is impregnable (منيع), nothing overcomes Him; He is precise (متقن) in His action, doing only what His wisdom (حكمته) necessitates. The sentence is an explanatory justification (بيان تعليلي) for His saying: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ etc., just as it is an explanatory justification for His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ etc. It is as if it were said: He sent down tranquility for such-and-forth, and that belongs to Him because to Him belong all the hosts and causes, because He is the All-mighty in an absolute sense and the All-wise in an absolute sense.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُدْخِلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ — "That He may admit the faithful men and women into gardens with streams running in them" (48:5) until the end of the verse. This is another justification for His saying: أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ in terms of meaning, just as His saying: لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً is a justification for it in terms of wording. It is as if it were said: He singled out the believers for the sending down of tranquility and deprived others of it so that the faith of these might increase along with their faith, and the reality of that is that He admits these into Paradise and punishes those. Thus, His saying لِيُدْخِلَ (that He may admit) is a substitute (بدل) or an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying لِيَزْدادُوا etc.

Regarding the object of the lam in لِيُدْخِلَ etc., there are other views, such as the view that it is linked to His saying فَتَحْنا (We have inaugurated), or His saying لِيَزْدادُوا (that they might enhance), or to all that preceded, among other views that are of no use to mention.

The joining of the believing women to the believing men in the verse is to dispel the illusion that Paradise and the expiation of sins are exclusive to males because the verse occurs in the context of the discourse on Jihad (الجهاد), and Jihad and victory were achieved at their hands. Thus, He explicitly named the believing women to dispel the illusion, as has been said.

The pronouns in خالِدِينَ (abiding) and يُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ (acquit them of their evils) refer to both the believing men and women together by way of predominance (التغليب).

His saying: وَكانَ ذلِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً — "and that is a great triumph with Allah" (48:5). This is an explanation that this is true happiness (سعادة حقيقية) about which there is no doubt, because it is so with Allah, and He speaks the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُعَذِّبَ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْمُنافِقاتِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ وَالْمُشْرِكاتِ — "And that He may punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women" (48:6) until the end of the verse. This is coordinated with His saying لِيُدْخِلَ (that He may admit) in the sense previously mentioned. The precedence of the hypocrites, men and women, over the polytheists, men and women, in the verse is because they are more harmful to the Muslims than the people of polytheism, and because the punishment of the people of hypocrisy is more severe. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ — "Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire" (4:145).

His saying: الظَّانِّينَ بِاللهِ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ — "who entertain evil thoughts about Allah" (48:6). Evil (السوء) with a fathah and then a sukun is a verbal noun meaning vileness (القبح), and al-su' (السوء) with a dammah is a noun. The "evil thought" (ظن السوء) is their thought that Allah would not help His Messenger. It has been said: what is meant by "evil thought" is what encompasses that and the rest of their evil thoughts of polytheism and disbelief.

His saying: عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ — "Adverse turns shall beset them" (48:6). This is a supplication against them or a decree against them; i.e., let them be harmed by the circle of evil that turns to strike whomever it strikes with destruction and punishment.

His saying: وَغَضِبَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَنَهُمْ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ — "and Allah is at wrath with them and He has cursed them, and He has prepared hell for them" (48:6). This is coordinated with His saying عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ etc. His saying: وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً — "and it is an evil destination" (48:6) is an explanation of the vileness of their destination, just as His saying: وَكانَ ذلِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً is an explanation of the goodness of the destination of the people of faith.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth" (48:7). Its meaning has been mentioned. It is apparent that it is an explanatory justification for the two verses—namely His saying: لِيُدْخِلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ until His saying: وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ—in the same way that its like was previously an explanatory justification for His saying: أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ etc.

It has been said: its content is linked to the last verse, so it is a threat to them that they are in the grip of His power, so He will take vengeance upon them. The first view is more apparent.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Ibn Sinan, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The reason for the revelation of this verse and this great victory was that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in a dream to enter the Sacred Mosque, perform the circumambulation (طواف), and shave his head with those who shave. So he informed his companions and commanded them to go out, and they went out.

When he reached Dhu al-Hulayfah, they entered the state of ihram (أحرموا) for the umrah (العمرة) and drove the sacrificial animals (البدن)—and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) drove sixty-six camels. They entered ihram from Dhu al-Hulayfah, reciting the talbiyah (ملبين) for the umrah. Those among them who drove the sacrificial animals (الهدي) had them marked and covered.

When the news reached Quraysh, they sent Khalid ibn al-Walid with two hundred horsemen as an ambush to intercept the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He would confront him on the mountains. When they were at a certain point on the road, the time for the Dhuhr prayer arrived. Bilal gave the call to prayer, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) led the people in prayer. Khalid ibn al-Walid said: 'If we had attacked them while they were in prayer, we would have struck them, for they do not interrupt their prayer. But another prayer is coming to them now, which is dearer to them than the light of their eyes. When they enter the prayer, we will raid them.' Then Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the 'Prayer of Fear' (صلاة الخوف) in His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذا كُنْتَ فِيهِمْ فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ الصَّلاةَ (When you are among them and maintain the prayer for them) (4:102)."

He said: "On the second day, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reached al-Hudaybiyyah. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had been calling the desert Arabs to mobilize on his way, but none followed him. They were saying: 'Does Muhammad and his companions hope to enter the Sanctuary when Quraysh have attacked them in their own homes and killed them? Muhammad and his companions will never return to Medina.'" (The tradition continues).

In al-Majma': Ibn Abbas said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out intending Mecca. When he reached al-Hudaybiyyah, his she-camel stopped. He urged her, but she would not move and knelt down. His companions said: 'The she-camel has become stubborn (خلأت).' He said: 'This is not her habit, but she has been held back by what held back the Elephant.'

He called Umar ibn al-Khattab to send him to the people of Mecca to ask their permission to enter Mecca, complete his umrah, and sacrifice his animals. He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have no kinsman there, and I fear Quraysh because of my intense enmity toward them. But I will point you to a man who is more honored there than I: Uthman ibn Affan.' He said: 'You have spoken the truth.'

So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called Uthman and sent him to Abu Sufyan and the nobles of Quraysh to inform them that he had not come for war, but had only come as a visitor to this House, honoring its sanctity. Quraysh detained him. Word reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Muslims that Uthman had been killed. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'We shall not leave until we have engaged the people in battle.' He called the people to the pledge. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood by the tree, leaned against it, and the people pledged to fight the polytheists and not to flee. Abdullah ibn Mughaffal said: 'I was standing at the head of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that day with a branch of a gum-acacia tree in my hand, fending [flies] off him while he took the people's pledge. He did not take their pledge for death, but rather he took their pledge that they would not flee.'"

Al-Zuhri, Urwah ibn al-Zubayr, and al-Miswar ibn Makhramah narrated: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out from Medina with some thirteen hundred of his companions. When they were at Dhu al-Hulayfah, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) garlanded the sacrificial animals, marked them, and entered ihram for the umrah. He sent ahead of him a scout of his from the Khuza'ah tribe to inform him about Quraysh.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traveled until he was at Ghadir al-Ashtat, near Usfan. His scout from Khuza'ah came to him and said: 'I left Ka'b ibn Lu'ay and Amir ibn Lu'ay having gathered the surrounding tribes (الأحابيش) for you; they have gathered masses and they will fight you or engage you in battle and bar you from the House.' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Depart.' So they departed until they were at a certain point on the road. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Khalid ibn al-Walid is at al-Ghamim with a cavalry of Quraysh as a vanguard, so take the path to the right.'

He traveled until he was at the mountain pass (الثنية), where his mount knelt down. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Al-Qaswa' has not become stubborn, but she has been held back by what held back the Elephant.' Then he said: 'By Allah, they shall not ask me for any plan in which they honor the sanctities of Allah except that I will grant it to them.' Then he urged her, and she sprang up with him.

He turned aside until he reached the furthest part of al-Hudaybiyyah at a small water-hole (ثمد) with little water, which the people were only sipping slowly. They complained to him of thirst. He pulled an arrow from his quiver and ordered them to place it in the water. By Allah, it did not cease gushing for them with plenty until they had quenched their thirst and departed from it.

While they were in that state, Budayl ibn Warqa' al-Khuza'i came to them with a group from Khuza'ah—and they were the confidants (عيبة نصح) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the people of Tihamah. He said: 'I left Ka'b ibn Lu'ay and Amir ibn Lu'ay with nursing camels (العوذ المطافيل); they will fight you and bar you from the House.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'We have not come to fight anyone; we have come to perform umrah. Quraysh have been exhausted by war and it has harmed them. If they wish, I will grant them a term of truce and they shall leave me and the people alone. If they wish to enter into what the people have entered into, they may do so; otherwise, they have rested. If they refuse, then by Him in Whose hand is my soul, I will surely fight them over this matter of mine until my neck is severed or Allah carries out His command.' Budayl said: 'I will convey to them what you say.'

He went until he came to Quraysh and said: 'We have come to you from this man, and he says such-and-forth.' Urwah ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi stood up and said: 'He has offered you a plan of right conduct, so accept it and let me go to him.' They said: 'Go to him.' So he went to him and began speaking to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him something similar to what he had said to Budayl.

Urwah then said: 'O Muhammad, have you considered if you were to uproot your people? Have you ever heard of anyone among the Arabs who destroyed his own root before you? And if it be the other way, then by Allah, I see faces and I see a mixed rabble (أشاباً) of people who are likely to flee and leave you.' Abu Bakr said to him: 'Go suck the clitoris of al-Lat! Shall we flee from him and leave him?' He asked: 'Who is this?' They said: 'Abu Bakr.' He said: 'By Him in Whose hand is my soul, were it not for a favor you did me for which I have not repaid you, I would have answered you.'

He began speaking to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and every time he spoke to him, he would reach for his beard. Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah was standing at the head of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a sword and wearing a helmet. Every time Urwah reached his hand toward the beard of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he would strike his hand with the scabbard of the sword and say: 'Take your hand away from the beard of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before it does not return to you.' He asked: 'Who is this?' They said: 'Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah.' He said: 'O traitor! Am I not still striving to resolve your treachery?'

Al-Mughirah had accompanied some people in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية), killed them, and taken their wealth. Then he came and embraced Islam. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'As for Islam, we have accepted it. As for the wealth, it is the wealth of treachery; we have no need for it.'

Then Urwah began to observe the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded them, they rushed to his command. When he performed ablution (توضأ), they almost fought over his ablution water. When they spoke, they lowered their voices in his presence, and they would not look him in the eye out of reverence for him.

Urwah returned to his companions and said: 'O people, by Allah, I have visited kings; I have visited Caesar, Khosrow, and the Negus. By Allah, I have never seen a king whose companions revere him as the companions of Muhammad revere Muhammad. When he commands them, they rush to his command. When he performs ablution, they almost fight over his ablution water. When they speak, they lower their voices in his presence, and they do not look him in the eye out of reverence for him. He has offered you a plan of right conduct, so accept it.'

A man from the Banu Kinanah said: 'Let me go to him.' They said: 'Go to him.' When he appeared before them, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'This is so-and-so, and he is from a people who revere the sacrificial animals, so send them out.' They were sent out for him, and the people met him reciting the talbiyah. When he saw that, he said: 'Glory be to Allah! It is not right that these should be barred from the House.'

A man called Mikraz ibn Hafs stood up and said: 'Let me go to him.' They said: 'Go to him.' When he appeared before them, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'This is Mikraz, and he is a wicked man.' He began speaking to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). While he was speaking to him, Suhayl ibn Amr came. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Your matter has become easy (سهل) for you.' He said: 'Write a document between us and you.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called Ali ibn Abi Talib. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'Write: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful (بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم).' Suhayl said: 'As for "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن), by Allah, I do not know what it is. But write: "In Your Name, O Allah" (باسمك اللهم).' The Muslims said: 'By Allah, we will not write except "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful."' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Write: "In Your Name, O Allah. This is what Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, has agreed upon."' Suhayl said: 'If we knew you were the Messenger of Allah, we would not have barred you from the House nor fought you. But write: "Muhammad ibn Abdillah."' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I am indeed the Messenger of Allah even if you call me a liar.' Then he said to Ali: 'Erase "Messenger of Allah."' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, my hand will not move to erase your name from prophethood.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took it and erased it.

Then he said: 'Write: This is what Muhammad ibn Abdillah and Suhayl ibn Amr have agreed upon. They have made peace on the condition of laying down war for ten years, during which the people shall be safe and refrain from [attacking] one another. And that whoever comes to Mecca from the companions of Muhammad as a pilgrim for Hajj or umrah or seeking the bounty of Allah, he shall be safe in his blood and wealth. And whoever comes to Medina from Quraysh passing through to Egypt or Syria, he shall be safe in his blood and wealth. And that between us there shall be a closed chest (عيبة مكفوفة)—meaning a heart pure from malice and deception—and that there shall be no theft (إسلال) nor betrayal (إغلال). And that whoever wishes to enter into the pact and covenant of Muhammad may do so, and whoever wishes to enter into the pact and covenant of Quraysh may do so.'

Khuza'ah sprang up and said: 'We are in the pact and covenant of Muhammad.' Banu Bakr sprang up and said: 'We are in the pact and covenant of Quraysh.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'On the condition that you leave the way clear between us and the House so we may perform circumambulation.' Suhayl said: 'By Allah, the Arabs shall not say that we were taken by force, but that shall be from next year.' So it was written. Suhayl said: 'On the condition that no man comes to you from us, even if he is upon your religion, except that you return him to us. And whoever comes to us from those with you, we shall not return him to you.' The Muslims said: 'Glory be to Allah! How can he be returned to the polytheists when he has come as a Muslim?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Whoever goes to them from us, may Allah distance him. And whoever comes to us from them, we shall return him to them; for if Allah knows Islam to be in his heart, He will make a way out for him.'

Suhayl said: 'And on the condition that you return from us this year of yours and do not enter Mecca upon us. When next year comes, we will leave it for you, and you shall enter it with your companions and stay for three days. You shall not enter it with weapons except swords in scabbards (القراب) and the weapon of the rider. And that these sacrificial animals shall be sacrificed wherever we have held them back; you shall not bring them forward to us.' He said: 'We drive them and you turn them back.'

While they were in that state, Abu Jandal ibn Suhayl ibn Amr came hobbling (يرسف) in his chains, having escaped from the lower part of Mecca, until he threw himself among the Muslims. Suhayl said: 'This, O Muhammad, is the first thing I hold you to in our agreement: that you return him.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'We have not yet concluded the document.' He said: 'By Allah, then I will never make peace with you on anything.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Then grant him to me.' He said: 'I will not grant him to you.' He said: 'Yes, do so.' He said: 'I will not.' Mikraz said: 'Yes, we have granted him.' Abu Jandal ibn Suhayl said: 'O assembly of Muslims! Am I to be returned to the polytheists when I have come as a Muslim? Do you not see what I have suffered?'—and he had been severely tortured.

Umar ibn al-Khattab said: 'By Allah, I never doubted since I embraced Islam except on that day. I went to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "Are you not the Prophet of Allah?" He said: "Yes." I said: "Are we not upon the truth and our enemy upon falsehood?" He said: "Yes." I said: "Then why should we give the low hand (الدنية) in our religion?" He said: "I am the Messenger of Allah, and I do not disobey Him, and He is my helper." I said: "Did you not used to tell us that we would come to the House and perform circumambulation truly?" He said: "Yes, but did I inform you that we would come to it this year?" I said: "No." He said: "Then you shall indeed come to it and perform circumambulation around it."' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sacrificed a camel, called for his barber, and shaved his hair. Then believing women came to him, and Allah the Exalted revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا جاءَكُمُ الْمُؤْمِناتُ مُهاجِراتٍ — "O you who have faith! When faithful women come to you as immigrants" (60:10)."

Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Yasar said: Buraydah ibn Sufyan narrated to me from Muhammad ibn Ka‘b: "The scribe of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله during this peace treaty was ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: 'Write: This is what Muhammad ibn ‘Abdullah has agreed upon with Suhayl ibn ‘Amr.' ‘Ali began to hesitate and refused to write anything except 'Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah.' The Messenger of Allah said: 'Indeed, you shall have the like of this—you shall give it while you are oppressed.' So he wrote what they [the pagans] demanded.

Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله returned to Medina. Abu Basir, a man from Quraysh who was a Muslim, came to him. They [the Quraysh] sent two men in pursuit of him and said: 'The treaty you made with us [stipulates his return].' So he handed him over to the two men. They set out with him until they reached Dhu al-Hulayfah, where they stopped to eat some of their dates. Abu Basir said to one of the two men: 'By Allah, I see your sword is very fine.' The man drew it and said: 'Indeed, it is fine; I have tested it time and again.' Abu Basir said: 'Let me look at it.' The man handed it to him, and Abu Basir struck him with it until he was dead (برد). The other man fled until he reached Medina; he entered the Mosque running. When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw him, He said: 'This man has seen something terrifying.' When he reached the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he said: 'By Allah, my companion has been killed, and I am surely to be killed too.'

Then Abu Basir arrived and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Allah has fulfilled your obligation (ذمتك); you returned me to them, and then Allah delivered me from them.' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Woe to his mother! A stirrer of war (مسعر حرب), if only he had someone with him.' When Abu Basir heard this, he realized that the Prophet would return him to them, so he left until he reached the seacoast.

Abu Jandal ibn Suhayl also escaped from them and joined Abu Basir. Thereafter, no man from Quraysh embraced Islam except that he joined Abu Basir, until a band gathered around him. By Allah, whenever they heard of a caravan of Quraysh heading for the Levant, they intercepted it, killed them, and took their wealth. Consequently, Quraysh sent word to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, imploring him by Allah and the ties of kinship to send for them, [stating] that whoever comes to him from them shall be secure. So the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent for them, and they came to him."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in a long narration whose beginning we cited at the start of this discussion, it is stated: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to his companions after the treaty was written: 'Sacrifice your camels and shave your heads.' They refused and said: 'How can we sacrifice and shave when we have not circumambulated the House nor performed the sa‘y between al-Safa and al-Marwah?' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله became distressed and complained of this to Umm Salamah. She said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you sacrifice and shave [first].' So the Messenger of Allah sacrificed and shaved. Then the people sacrificed, [though they were] between certainty, doubt, and hesitation."

I say: This is narrated in other reports through both Shi‘i and Sunni chains. What al-Tabarsi narrated is taken, with some summarization, from what al-Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and al-Nasa’i narrated from Marwan and al-Miswar.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bayhaqi extracted from ‘Urwah who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله approached from al-Hudaybiyyah, returning [to Medina]. A man from the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'By Allah, this is no victory! We were barred from the House, our sacrificial animals were prevented [from reaching their destination], the Messenger of Allah stayed at al-Hudaybiyyah, and he returned two Muslim men who had come out [to join him].'

When the saying of some of his companions—that this was not a victory—reached the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, He said: 'What evil speech! Rather, this is the greatest victory. The polytheists were satisfied to push you away from their land with ease (بالراح), asking you for a settlement and desiring your return [to Medina]. They have seen from you what they disliked, yet Allah has granted you triumph over them and returned you safe, successful, and rewarded. This is indeed the greatest victory.

Have you forgotten the day of Uhud, when you were climbing [the mountain] without twisting around for anyone, while I was calling you from your rear? Have you forgotten the day of the Confederates (الأحزاب), when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when the eyes rolled and the hearts reached the throats, and you were harboring [uncertain] thoughts about Allah?'

The Muslims said: 'Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth; it is indeed the greatest of victories. By Allah, O Prophet of Allah, we did not think of what you thought of, and you are surely more knowledgeable of Allah and of affairs than we are.' Then Allah revealed Surah al-Fath."

I say: The narrations regarding the story of al-Hudaybiyyah are numerous, and what we have cited is but a portion of them.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to ‘Umar ibn Yazid, the seller of Sabiri cloth, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdullah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah in His Book: لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ (that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come). He said: 'He had no sin, nor did He ever intend a sin. However, Allah made Him bear the sins of His followers (شيعته) and then forgave them.'"

In al-‘Uyun, regarding the gathering of al-Rida with al-Ma’mun, with its chain to Ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the gathering of al-Ma’mun while al-Rida عليه‌السلام was with him. al-Ma’mun said: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your position that the Prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He replied: 'Yes.' —until he said— al-Ma’mun asked: 'Then inform me regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ (that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come).'

Imam al-Rida عليه‌السلام said: 'There was no one in the eyes of the polytheists of Mecca whose sin (ذنب) was greater than that of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, because they used to worship three hundred and sixty idols besides Allah. When He came to them with the call to the word of sincerity (كلمة الإخلاص), it was grievous and momentous to them. They said: "Has he reduced the gods to a single god? This is indeed an amazing thing!" and their elite went about [saying]: "Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed a thing intended. We did not hear of this in the latter-day creed. This is nothing but a fabrication." When Allah granted His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the conquest of Mecca, He said: "O Muhammad, إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُبِينًا * لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ (Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come)"—meaning, in the eyes of the polytheists of Mecca, due to your calling to the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah in the past and in the future. This is because some of the polytheists of Mecca embraced Islam, some left Mecca, and those who remained were unable to deny monotheism when He called the people to it. Thus, His "sin" in their eyes became "forgiven" by His triumph over them.' al-Ma’mun said: 'May Allah reward you, O Abu al-Hasan!'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Mansur ibn Hazim, from Abu ‘Abdullah عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not cease [saying]: إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (Indeed I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous day) until Surah al-Fath was revealed, after which He did not return to that saying."

I say: This meaning is also narrated through Sunni chains. However, the narration is not without its issues, as it is based on the assumption that "sin" (الذنب) in the verse refers to "disobedience" (المعصية) which would contradict infallibility (العصمة).

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Jamil, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdullah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (It is He who sent down composure into the hearts of the faithful). He said: '[It is] faith (الإيمان).' Then He, the Mightiest of speakers, said: لِيَزْدَادُوا إِيمَانًا مَعَ إِيمَانِهِمْ (that they might add faith to their faith)."

I say: The apparent sense of the narration is that he عليه‌السلام took the saying of the Exalted in the verse: لِيَزْدَادُوا إِيمَانًا مَعَ إِيمَانِهِمْ (that they might add faith to their faith) as an explanation for "composure" (السكينة). There are other narrations with the same meaning.

In the same source, with its chain from Abu ‘Amr al-Zubayri from Abu ‘Abdullah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I said to him: 'O learned one, inform me which deeds are best in the sight of Allah?' He said: 'That without which Allah accepts nothing.' I asked: 'And what is that?' He said: 'Faith (الإيمان) in Allah, besides whom there is no god; it is the highest of deeds in rank, the noblest in station, and the most magnificent in portion.'

I said: 'Will you not inform me about faith—is it both word and deed, or word without deed?' He said: 'Faith is deed (عمل) in its entirety, and the word (القول) is part of that deed, by an obligation from Allah made clear in His Book—its light is manifest, its proof is established; the Book testifies to it and calls toward it.' I said: 'Describe it for me, may I be your ransom, so that I may understand it.' He said: 'Faith has states (حالات), degrees (درجات), attributes (صفات), and stations (منازل). Among it is that which is complete and has reached its perfection, that which is deficient and its deficiency is evident, and that which is superior and its superiority is increasing.'

I said: 'Does faith really become complete, diminish, and increase?' He said: 'Yes.' I asked: 'How is that?' He said: 'Because Allah, Blessed and Exalted, enjoined faith upon the limbs of the son of Adam, divided it among them, and distributed it within them. There is no limb among his limbs except that it has been entrusted with a part of faith different from what its sister-limb has been entrusted with. Thus, whoever meets Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, having guarded his limbs and fulfilled for every one of his limbs what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, enjoined upon it, shall meet Allah with his faith perfected, and he is of the people of Paradise. But whoever betrays anything of them or transgresses what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded regarding them, shall meet Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, with deficient faith.'

I said: 'I have understood the deficiency of faith and its completeness, but from where does its increase come?' He said: 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذَا مَا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَذِهِ إِيمَانًا فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَانًا وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ * وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ (Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did this increase in faith?’ As for those who have faith, it increases them in faith, and they rejoice. But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness, it only adds profanity to their profanity); and He said: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ آمَنُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ وَزِدْنَاهُمْ هُدًى (We relate to you their adventure truly: they were indeed a group of youths who believed in their Lord, and We increased them in guidance).

If it were all a single [level] with no increase or decrease, no one among them would have merit over another, the blessings therein would be equal, the people would be the same, and preference (التفضيل) would be nullified. However, by the completeness of faith, the believers enter Paradise; by the increase in faith, the believers excel one another in degrees before Allah; and by deficiency, the negligent enter the Fire.'"

Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (٨)
Indeed We have sent you as a witness, and as a bearer of good news and warner, (8)
لِّتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ وَتُوَقِّرُوهُ وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٩)
that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and that you may support him and revere him, and that you may glorify Him morning and evening. (9)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَايِعُونَكَ إِنَّمَا يُبَايِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ فَمَن نَّكَثَ فَإِنَّمَا يَنكُثُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِمَا عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ ٱللَّهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٠)
Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands. Then whosoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own detriment, and whoever fulfills the covenant he has made with Allah, He will give him a great reward. (10)

(Commentary)

This is a second section of the verses of the Surah, in which the Glorified One introduces His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to him with an introduction of exaltation and magnification—stating that He has sent him as a witness, a bearer of good news, and a warner; that obedience to him is obedience to Allah, and pledging allegiance to him is pledging allegiance to Allah. The first section was an expression of favor from the Exalted upon His Prophet through the manifest victory, forgiveness, the completion of blessing, guidance, and support, and upon the believers by sending down tranquility (السكينة) — the divine peace and steadfastness that anchors the heart — into their hearts and admitting them into Paradise, while threatening the polytheists and hypocrites with wrath, curse, and the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَمُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً "Indeed We have sent you as a witness, and as a bearer of good news and warner" (48:8). What is meant by his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله witnessing (الشهادة) is his witnessing over deeds—including faith, disbelief, and righteous or evil actions. The mention of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله witnessing has been repeated in the speech of the Exalted, and the full discussion regarding the meaning of this witnessing has preceded; it is a witnessing of bearing (حمل) in this world and delivery (أداء) in the Hereafter.

His being a bearer of good news (مبشر) refers to his bringing glad tidings to those who believe and are God-conscious (اتقى) of proximity to Allah and His magnificent reward. His being a warner (نذير) refers to his warning and frightening those who disbelieve and turn away with His painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ وَتُوقِّرُوهُ وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلاً "That you may believe in Allah and His Apostle, and help him and honour him, and glorify Him morning and evening" (48:9). The famous recitation uses the second-person address (تاء الخطاب) in the four verbs. Ibn Kathir and Abu Amr recited using the third-person masculine plural (ياء الغيبة) in all of them, and their recitation is more weightier in view of the context.

In any case, the lam in "that you may believe" (لِتُؤْمِنُوا) is for causality (التعليل); meaning: We sent you as such-and-such so that they may believe in Allah and His Messenger.

Assistance (التعزير) — according to what has been said — is support (النصر), and honoring (التوقير) is magnification (التعظيم), as the Exalted said: ما لَكُمْ لا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقاراً "What is the matter with you that you do not look for majesty in Allah?" (71:13). It appears that the pronouns in "help Him" (تُعَزِّرُوهُ), "honour Him" (تُوقِّرُوهُ), and "glorify Him" (تُسَبِّحُوهُ) all refer to Allah the Exalted. The meaning is: We have sent you as such-and-such so that they may believe in Allah and His Messenger, and support Him (the Exalted) with their hands and tongues, and magnify Him, and glorify Him — which refers to prayer (الصلاة) — morning and evening (بكرة وأصيلا), i.e., at dawn and dusk.

It has also been said that the two pronouns in "help him" and "honour him" refer to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, while the pronoun in "glorify Him" refers to Allah the Exalted. However, this is weakened by the necessity of having inconsistent pronouns within a coordinated sequence.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ يَدُ اللهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ "Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands" (48:10) to the end of the verse. Allegiance (البيعة) is a type of covenant (الميثاق) involving the offering of obedience. It is stated in al-Mufradat: "One pledges allegiance (بايع) to the ruler when he undertakes to offer obedience to him in exchange for what is allotted to him." The word is derived from "selling" (البيع) in its well-known sense; it was their custom that when they wished to finalize a sale, the seller would give his hand to the buyer. It was as if they were symbolizing the transfer of ownership by transferring the power of disposal — most of which is realized by the hand — to the buyer through the clasping of hands (التصفيق). Thus, the clasping of hands when offering obedience was named bay'ah (بيعة) and mubaya'ah (مبايعة). Its essential meaning is the one pledging allegiance giving his hand to the ruler, for instance, so that he may do with him as he wishes.

Thus, His saying: "Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah" places the pledge of allegiance to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in the position of the pledge of allegiance to the Exalted, based on the claim that it is one and the same. Therefore, the offering of obedience they confront him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with is nothing other than what they confront Allah the Glorified with, because obedience to him is obedience to Allah. Then He affirmed this further and emphasized it with His saying: "the hand of Allah is above their hands," whereby He made his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله hand the hand of Allah, just as He made his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله throwing the throwing of His own Self in His saying: وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ رَمى "And you did not throw when you threw, rather it was Allah who threw" (8:17).

There are many verses attributing the status of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to His own Self, such as the saying of the Exalted: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ "Whoever obeys the Apostle has certainly obeyed Allah" (4:80), and His saying: فَإِنَّهُمْ لا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآياتِ اللهِ يَجْحَدُونَ "Yet it is not you that they deny, but it is the signs of Allah that the wrongdoers impugn" (6:33), and His saying: لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128).

And His saying: فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ "So whoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own detriment" (48:10). Breaking (النكث) is the violation of a covenant or pledge of allegiance. The sentence is a derivation from His saying: "Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah." The meaning is: Since your pledge of allegiance is the pledge of allegiance to Allah, then the one who breaks and violates it only violates the pledge of Allah, and none shall be harmed by that except his own soul, just as none shall benefit from fulfillment except his own soul, for Allah is independent of all the worlds (العالمين).

And His saying: وَمَنْ أَوْفى بِما عاهَدَ عَلَيْهُ اللهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً "And whoever fulfills what he has pledged to Allah, He will give him a great reward" (48:10) is a beautiful promise for keeping the covenant and fulfilling it.

The verse is not without an allusion to the fact that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, during the pledge of allegiance, would place his hand upon their hands; thus his hand was above their hands, not the reverse.

The commentators have other views regarding His saying: "the hand of Allah is above their hands."

It has been said: It is a form of imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية) and metaphor by metonymy (استعارة بالكناية), brought to emphasize what preceded it and to establish that the pledge of allegiance to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is like the pledge of allegiance to Allah without any difference. Thus, it was imagined that the Glorified is like one of the human parties to a pledge, and so a hand was affirmed for Him that falls above the hands of those pledging to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in place of the Messenger's hand. However, this is unsuitable for the presence of His sanctity (القدس), the Exalted, to be imagined in a manner from which He is transcendent.
