It has also been said: What is meant by "hand" (اليد) is power and support; meaning the power and support of Allah are above their power and support—i.e., trust in the support of Allah, not in their support.

The flaw in this is that the context is one of magnifying the pledge of allegiance to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and that their pledging to him is a pledge to Allah. While trusting in Allah and His support is good in every state, it is foreign to this specific context.

It has also been said: What is meant by "hand" is the gift and the blessing (النعمة); meaning Allah's blessing upon them with reward or by granting them the success to pledge allegiance to you is above their blessing upon you by pledging allegiance. It was also said: His blessing upon them with guidance is greater than their blessing upon you with obedience—among other views they have mentioned which are of no avail.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Adi, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Khatib, and Ibn Asakir in his Tarikh narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: "When this verse وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ (and help him) was revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to his companions: 'What is that?' They said: 'Allah and His Messenger know best.' He said: 'That you support him (لتنصروه).'"

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission to Abd al-Salam ibn Salih al-Harawi, who said: "I said to Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه‌السلام: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah—what do you say regarding the hadith narrated by the people of hadith: that the believers visit their Lord from their stations in Paradise?' He said: 'O Abu al-Salt, indeed Allah the Exalted favored His Prophet Muhammad—over all His creation of prophets and angels, and He made obedience to him His obedience, pledging allegiance to him His pledging allegiance, and visiting him in this world and the Hereafter His visiting. For the Mighty and Majestic said: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ (Whoever obeys the Apostle has certainly obeyed Allah), and He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ يَدُ اللهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ (Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands). And the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Whoever visits me in my life or after my death has certainly visited Allah."

His rank in Paradise is the highest of ranks, and whoever visits him in his rank in Paradise from his own station—has certainly visited Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He.'"

In al-Irshad of al-Mufid, in the account of the pledge of allegiance to al-Rida عليه‌السلام, it is stated: "Al-Ma'mun sat and placed two large cushions for al-Rida عليه‌السلام—until he reached his own seat and carpet—and he seated al-Rida عليه‌السلام in the presence wearing a turban and a sword. Then he ordered his son, al-Abbas ibn al-Ma'mun, to be the first of the people to pledge allegiance to him. Al-Rida عليه‌السلام raised his hand such that its back faced his own face and its palm faced their faces. Al-Ma'mun said to him: 'Extend your hand for the pledge.' Al-Rida عليه‌السلام replied: 'This is how the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to take the pledge.' So the people pledged allegiance to him while his hand was above their hands."

Ayahs

سَيَقُولُ لَكَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ شَغَلَتْنَآ أَمْوَٰلُنَا وَأَهْلُونَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعًۢا ۚ بَلْ كَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًۢا (١١)
The Bedouins who had stayed back [from joining the Prophet in his ‘umrah journey to Makkah] will tell you, ‘Our possessions and families kept us occupied. So plead [to Allah] for our forgiveness!’ They will say with their tongues what is not in their hearts. Say, ‘Whether He desires to cause you harm or desires to bring you benefit, who can be of any avail to you against Allah[’s will]? Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do.’ (11)
بَلْ ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَنقَلِبَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَدًا وَزُيِّنَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَظَنَنتُمْ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا (١٢)
Rather, you thought that the Apostle and the faithful will not ever return to their folks, and that was made to seem decorous to your hearts; you entertained evil thoughts, and you were a ruined lot. (12)
وَمَن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ فَإِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَعِيرًا (١٣)
Those who have no faith in Allah and His Apostle [should know that] We have prepared a blaze for the faithless. (13)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٤)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (14)
سَيَقُولُ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا ٱنطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ ۖ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٥)
[In the near future] when you will set out to capture booty, those who stayed behind [in this journey] will say, : ‘Let us follow you.’ They desire to change the word of Allah. Say, ‘You will never follow us! Allah has said thus beforehand.’ Then they will say, ‘You are envious of us.’ Indeed, they do not understand but a little. (15)
قُل لِّلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقَـٰتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ ۖ فَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا ۖ وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ كَمَا تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٦)
Say to the Bedouins who stayed behind, ‘[Later on] you will be called against a people of a great might: they will either embrace Islam, or you will fight them. So if you obey, Allah will give you a good reward; but if you turn away like you turned away before, He will punish you with a painful punishment.’ (16)
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٧)
There is no blame on the blind, nor is there any blame on the lame, nor is there blame on the sick [if they are unable to go out with the troops to face the enemies]; and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He will admit him into gardens with streams running in them, and whoever refuses to comply, He will punish him with a painful punishment. (17)

(Commentary)

This is a third section of verses addressing the state of the desert Arabs (الأعراب) who remained behind and did not accompany the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on his journey to al-Hudaybiyyah. They did not march forth when he called upon them to do so. It is said they were desert Arabs dwelling around Medina from the tribes of Juhaynah, Muzaynah, Ghifar, Ashja‘, Aslam, and al-Dil. They stayed behind and did not accompany the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: "Muhammad and those with him are going to a people who fought them only yesterday in the heart of their own territory and inflicted a crushing defeat upon them; they will certainly never return from this journey, nor will they ever go back to their homes and families."

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, informed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in these verses that they would meet him and offer excuses for staying behind, claiming they were occupied with their possessions and families, and they would ask him to seek Allah’s forgiveness for them. Allah exposed their lie in what they said, mentioning that the actual reason for their staying behind was something else—namely, their evil thoughts. He further informed him that they would ask to join him later, but they would not be permitted to do so; however, they would eventually be called to fight another people. If they obeyed, they would have a generous reward, but if they turned away, they would face a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَقُولُ لَكَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ شَغَلَتْنا أَمْوالُنا وَأَهْلُونا فَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا — "The ones of the desert Arabs who were left behind will say to you, ‘Our possessions and our families kept us busy, so plead [to Allah] for our forgiveness’" (48:11), until the end of the verse. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "The one left behind (المخلف) is the person left in a place after those departing from the town have gone; it is derived from 'behind' (الخلف), and its opposite is 'the one sent forward' (المقدم)." End quote. The desert Arabs (الأعراب)—according to what has been said—refers to the group of Arabs from the wilderness (البادية), and the term is not applied to the urban Arabs (الحاضرة); it is a collective noun (اسم جمع) that has no singular form from its own root.

His saying: سَيَقُولُ لَكَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ — "The ones of the desert Arabs who were left behind will say to you" is an announcement of what they would say to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the future. The phrasing contains an indication that these verses were revealed during his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return from al-Hudaybiyyah to Medina, before he had reached it.

His saying: شَغَلَتْنا أَمْوالُنا وَأَهْلُونا فَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا — "Our possessions and our families kept us busy, so plead [to Allah] for our forgiveness" means that the preoccupation that prevented us from accompanying you and marching out with you was our wealth and our families, as there was no one to look after our affairs, and we feared their loss; thus, we stayed with them. Therefore, seek forgiveness for us from Allah, the Exalted, that He may forgive our staying behind. Their request for forgiveness is evidence that they viewed staying behind as a sin (الذنب); thus, their claim of being occupied by wealth and family was not an excuse to absolve themselves of the sin, but rather a mention of the cause that led them into the sin.

His saying: يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ ما لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ — "They say with their tongues what is not in their hearts" is a refutation of everything they reported and requested. It was not the case that their preoccupation with wealth and family was what kept them back, nor were they truly concerned about his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking forgiveness for them. Rather, they only requested it to serve as a shield (جنة) by which they could deflect blame and reproach from themselves.

His saying: قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً إِنْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعاً — "Say, ‘Who can avail you anything against Allah should He desire to visit you with some harm or desire to bring you some benefit?’" (48:11) is a definitive response to the excuse they offered regarding their preoccupation with wealth and family. Its essence is that to Allah, the Glorified, belongs the creation and the command, and He is the Master and Disposer of all things; there is no Lord besides Him. Thus, there is no harm or benefit except by His will and volition. No one possesses anything against Allah, the Exalted, such that they could compel Him to withhold harm or perform good if He intended harm, or to withhold a good if He intended something other than what this hypothetical compeller desired. Since this is the case, your turning away from marching with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to support the religion, and your preoccupation with what you used as an excuse—the preservation of wealth and families—will avail you nothing against Allah; it will not repel harm if Allah intends harm for you, nor will it assist in bringing benefit or hastening it if He intends benefit for you.

Thus, His saying: قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ — "Say, ‘Who can avail you...’" etc., is a response to their excuse of being busy, even if their truthfulness in that claim were to be conceded. Its summary is that your reliance on outward causes (الأسباب)—including your own planning and staying behind from a religious project—to repel harm and attract benefit will avail you nothing regarding harm or benefit. Rather, the matter follows what Allah, the Glorified, has willed. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَنْ يُصِيبَنا إِلَّا ما كَتَبَ اللهُ لَنا — "Say, ‘Nothing will befall us except what Allah has ordained for us’" (9:51).

Adhering to causes (الأسباب) and not disregarding them, while legitimate and commanded, is only so when it does not conflict with what is more important, such as defending the Truth, even if it involves some potential hardships—unless it leads to a certain danger (خطر قطعي) where defense and effort would have no effect.

His saying: بَلْ كانَ اللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً — "Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do" (48:11) is an allusion directed at them, indicating their lie in saying: "Our possessions and our families kept us busy."

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْقَلِبَ الرَّسُولُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلى أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَداً وَزُيِّنَ ذلِكَ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ — "Rather you thought that the Apostle and the believers would never return to their families, and that was made to seem fair to your hearts" (48:12), etc., is an exposition of what was hinted at by His saying: "Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do," regarding their lying in their excuse. The meaning is: You did not stay behind because of being busy with wealth and families; rather, you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, and that those who went out would be killed at the hands of the Quraysh due to their large numbers, intense might, power, and capability. That is why you stayed behind.

His saying: وَزُيِّنَ ذلِكَ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ — "and that was made to seem fair to your hearts" means that Satan made that thought seem fair in your hearts, so you acted upon what that beautified thought dictated—which was to stay behind and not go out, for fear of perishing and being wiped out.

His saying: وَظَنَنْتُمْ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ وَكُنْتُمْ قَوْماً بُوراً — "and you entertained evil thoughts, and you were a lost people" (48:12). The term bur (البور)—according to what has been said—is a verbal noun meaning corruption (الفساد) or destruction (الهلاك), intended here in the sense of the active participle; meaning, you were a corrupt or doomed people.

It has been said that the "evil thought" (ظن السوء) refers to their thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families. However, it is not improbable that it refers to their thought that Allah would not help His Messenger nor make His religion manifest, as previously mentioned regarding His saying in the sixth verse of the Surah: الظَّانِّينَ بِاللهِ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ — "who entertain evil thoughts about Allah" (48:6); indeed, this is more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ فَإِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَعِيراً — "As for those who do not profess faith in Allah and His Apostle, We have indeed prepared a blaze for the faithless" (48:13). The combination in these verses between faith in Allah and His Messenger is to demonstrate that disbelieving in the Messenger by not obeying him is disbelief in Allah. The verse carries a tone of threat.

His saying: فَإِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَعِيراً — "We have indeed prepared a blaze for the faithless." The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "We have prepared it for them," but the explicit noun was placed instead of the pronoun to indicate the reason for the judgment by linking it to the derivative attribute (الكفر). The meaning is: We have prepared and readied for them, because of their disbelief, a blaze (سعيراً)—that is, a kindled and flaming fire. The indefiniteness of "blaze" (سعيراً) is for the purpose of inspiring awe and terror (التهويل).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً — "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (48:14). The meaning of the verse is clear and it serves as a support for what preceded. Ending the mention of absolute sovereignty (الملك المطلق) with the two names—the All-Forgiving (الغفور) and the Most Merciful (الرحيم)—is an indication that mercy precedes wrath, and an encouragement to seek forgiveness and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انْطَلَقْتُمْ إِلى مَغانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوها ذَرُونا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ — "The ones who were left behind will say, when you set out to take the spoils, ‘Let us follow you’" (48:15), until the end of the verse. This is an announcement that the believers would soon undertake a military expedition in which they would be granted victory and obtain spoils, and those who stayed behind would ask to be allowed to follow them out of greed for the booty. That expedition was the Battle of Khaybar; the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers passed by it, conquered it, and took the spoils. Allah, the Exalted, reserved those spoils exclusively for those who were with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on his journey to al-Hudaybiyyah, allowing no one else to share them.

The meaning is: You will set out for an expedition containing spoils that you will take, and these who stayed behind will say: "Leave us to follow you."

His saying: يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُبَدِّلُوا كَلامَ اللهِ — "They desire to change the word of Allah." It is said that this refers to His promise to the people of al-Hudaybiyyah that He would reserve the spoils of Khaybar for them after its conquest, as will come in His saying: وَعَدَكُمُ اللهُ مَغانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَها فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هذِهِ — "Allah has promised you many spoils which you will capture. So He has expedited this for you" (48:20). This is alluded to in the present verse by His saying: "when you set out to take the spoils."

His saying: قُلْ لَنْ تَتَّبِعُونا كَذلِكُمْ قالَ اللهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "Say, ‘You shall not follow us. Thus did Allah say before’" is a command from Him, the Exalted, to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to prevent them from following, based on what Allah, the Exalted, had said before they even requested to follow.

His saying: فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنا — "Then they will say, ‘Rather you envy us.’" That is, those who stayed behind will say, after being denied the following they requested: "Rather you envy us." His saying: بَلْ كانُوا لا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "In fact, they do not understand except a little" is a response to their saying: "Rather you envy us." The address was not directed to them personally because the claim is that they do not understand speech (الحديث); therefore, the response was directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: "In fact, they do not understand except a little."

This is because their saying: "Rather you envy us" is a rejection (إضراب) of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) statement to them by Allah’s command: "You shall not follow us. Thus did Allah say before." Thus, the meaning of their statement is: Our being prevented from following is not by a command from Allah; rather, you and those believers with you from al-Hudaybiyyah are preventing us so that we do not share the spoils with you, desiring to keep them exclusively for yourselves.

This is a statement that no believer possessing intellect and discernment would address to the infallible Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who neither acts nor speaks in any matter except by the command of Allah—unless it be out of extreme simplicity of mind and dullness of understanding. Thus, this statement with which they confronted the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), while they claimed faith and Islam, is the clearest evidence of their weak reasoning and lack of understanding (فقه).

From here, it becomes clear that the meaning of their not understanding "except a little" refers to the simplicity of their intellects and the weakness of their understanding of speech, not that they understand some speech and fail to understand other parts—which would be the majority—nor that some of them understand while most do not, as some have interpreted it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلى قَوْمٍ أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقاتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ — "Say to the ones of the desert Arabs who were left behind, ‘You will be called to [fight] a people of great might: you will fight them, or they will submit’" (48:16), etc. They have differed as to who these people are. It is said they are the Hawazin; it is said the Thaqif; it is said both Hawazin and Thaqif; it is said they are the Romans in the battles of Mu'tah and Tabuk; it is said they are the people of apostasy (أهل الردة) whom Abu Bakr fought after the passing [of the Prophet]; it is said they are the Persians; and it is said they are the desert Arabs and Kurds of Persia.

The apparent meaning of His saying: سَتُدْعَوْنَ — "You will be called" is that they are some of the peoples whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fought after the conquest of Khaybar, such as the Hawazin, Thaqif, and the Romans at Mu'tah. The previous saying of the Exalted: "You shall not follow us" refers to the denial of their following in the Battle of Khaybar, as suggested by the context.

His saying: تُقاتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ — "you will fight them, or they will submit" is a new sentence (استئناف) indicating a categorization (التنويع); meaning, either you fight them or they submit. This implies they are polytheists from whom tribute (الجزية) is not accepted, unlike the People of the Book; rather, they must either fight or embrace Islam.

It is not correct to take "you will fight them" (تُقاتِلُونَهُمْ) as an adjective for "people" (قوم), because they are called to the act of fighting the people, not called to a people who happen to be fighting them. Similarly, it is not correct to take it as a circumstantial clause (حال) for the pro-agent of "you will be called" (سَتُدْعَوْنَ), because they are called to fight the people, not called to them while they are already in the state of fighting them—as has been said.

Then the Glorified completed the discourse with a promise and a threat regarding obedience and disobedience, saying: فَإِنْ تُطِيعُوا — "So if you obey"—meaning by marching out to them—يُؤْتِكُمُ اللهُ أَجْراً حَسَناً — "Allah will give you a good reward," وَإِنْ تَتَوَلَّوْا — "but if you turn away"—meaning by disobedience and not marching out—كَما تَوَلَّيْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "as you turned away before"—when you did not go out on the journey to al-Hudaybiyyah—يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — "He will punish you with a painful punishment" (48:16)—meaning in this world, as is apparent from the context, or in both this world and the Hereafter together.

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمى حَرَجٌ وَلا عَلَى الْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ — "There is no blame on the blind, nor is there any blame on the lame, nor is there any blame on the sick" (48:17). This is a removal of the ruling of the obligation of Jihad from those with physical disabilities for whom Jihad is difficult, by removing its necessary consequence, which is blame (الحرج).

Then He completed the verse by repeating a similar conclusion to the previous verse, saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — "Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He will admit him into gardens with conduits running through them, but whoever turns away, He will punish him with a painful punishment" (48:17).

Ayahs

۞ لَّقَدْ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ فَعَلِمَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَثَـٰبَهُمْ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا (١٨)
Allah was certainly pleased with the faithful when they swore allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down composure on them, and requited them with a victory near at hand (18)
وَمَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٩)
and abundant spoils that they will capture, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (19)
وَعَدَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَهَا فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦ وَكَفَّ أَيْدِىَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنكُمْ وَلِتَكُونَ ءَايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٢٠)
Allah has promised you abundant spoils, which you will capture. He has expedited this one for you, and withheld men’s hands from you, so that it may be a sign for the faithful, and that He may guide you to a straight path. (20)
وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا قَدْ أَحَاطَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا (٢١)
And other [spoils as well] which you have not yet captured: Allah has comprehended them, and Allah has power over all things. (21)
وَلَوْ قَـٰتَلَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوَلَّوُا۟ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٢٢)
If the faithless fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee]. Then they will not find any protector or helper. (22)
سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٣)
[It is] Allah’s precedent that has passed before, and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent. (23)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُم بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا (٢٤)
It is He who withheld their hands from you, and your hands from them, in the valley of Makkah, after He had given you victory over them, and Allah sees best what you do. (24)
هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْهَدْىَ مَعْكُوفًا أَن يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُّؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَآءٌ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٌ لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَن تَطَـُٔوهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُم مِّنْهُم مَّعَرَّةٌۢ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۖ لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا۟ لَعَذَّبْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٢٥)
They are the ones who disbelieved and barred you from the Sacred Mosque, and kept the offering from reaching its destination. And were it not for [certain] faithful men and faithful women, whom you did not know—lest you should trample them, and thus the blame for [killing] them should fall on you unawares; [He held you back] so that Allah may admit into His mercy whomever He wishes. Had they been separate, We would have surely punished the faithless among them with a painful punishment. (25)
إِذْ جَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَحَقَّ بِهَا وَأَهْلَهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٢٦)
When the faithless nourished bigotry in their hearts, the bigotry of pagan ignorance, Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and the faithful, and made them abide by the word of Godwariness, for they were the worthiest of it and deserved it, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (26)
لَّقَدْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ ٱلرُّءْيَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ ۖ فَعَلِمَ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا۟ فَجَعَلَ مِن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا (٢٧)
Certainly Allah has fulfilled His Apostle’s vision in all truth: You will surely enter the Sacred Mosque, God willing, in safety and without any fear, with your heads shaven or hair cropped. So He knew what you did not know, and He assigned [you] besides that a victory near at hand. (27)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (٢٨)
It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion, that He may make it prevail over all religions, and Allah suffices as witness. (28)

(Commentary)

This is a fourth section of verses in which the Exalted mentions the believers among those who were with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) during his departure to al-Hudaybiyyah. He mentions His pleasure with them when they pledged allegiance to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) under the tree, then He bestows His favor upon them by sending down tranquility (السكينة) and rewarding them with a near victory and abundant spoils which they would take.

He also informs them—as a glad tiding—that if the polytheists had fought them, they would have surely turned their backs in retreat, and that the vision seen by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was a true vision: they would enter the Sacred Mosque in security, with their heads shaved, fearing not. For He, the Exalted, sent His Messenger with guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and the religion of truth (دين الحق) to make it prevail over all religions, even if the polytheists should detest it.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ — Allah was certainly pleased with the believers when they swore allegiance to you under the tree (48:18). Pleasure (الرضا) is a state that occurs to the soul upon encountering what accords with it and accepting it without resistance; its opposite is displeasure (السخط). When attributed to Allah, the Glorified, it refers to the granting of reward (الإثابة) and fair recompense, rather than a transient state or an accidental, originated attribute (الصفة العارضة الحادثة), as such is impossible for Him, the Exalted. Thus, His pleasure, the Glorified, is among the attributes of action (صفات الفعل), not the attributes of essence (صفات الذات).

Pleasure (الرضا)—as has been said—is used transitively to the object directly, transitively with the preposition ‘an (عن), and transitively with the preposition bi (ب). When it is transitive by itself, it may enter upon an essence, such as: "I was pleased with Zayd," or upon a meaning, such as: "I was pleased with the leadership of Zayd." The Exalted said: وَرَضيتُ لَكُمُ الإِسلامَ دينًا — and I have approved Islam for you as religion (5:3). When it is transitive with ‘an, it enters upon an essence, as in His saying: رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him (98:8). When it is transitive with bi, it enters upon a meaning, as in His saying, the Exalted: أَرَضيتُم بِالحَياةِ الدُّنيا مِنَ الآخِرَةِ — Are you pleased with the life of this world instead of the Hereafter? (9:38).

Since the pleasure attributed to Him, the Exalted, is an attribute of action in the sense of rewarding and recompensing, and recompense is only in exchange for an action rather than the essence itself, then in what is attributed of His pleasure to the essence and made transitive with ‘an—as in the verse: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ—there is a type of literary attention (عناية). This necessitated considering the pleasure, which is attached to the action, as being attached to the essence. This was done by taking their pledge of allegiance (بيعة)—which is the object of the pleasure—as the temporal circumstance (ظرف) for the pleasure, so the pleasure could not but be attached to their very selves.

Thus, His saying: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ is an announcement of His rewarding them in exchange for their pledge of allegiance to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) under the tree.

The pledge of allegiance on the day of al-Hudaybiyyah took place under a gum acacia tree (سمرة), where those believers with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) pledged their allegiance. It has become clear through this that the adverbial phrase (الظرف) in His saying: إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ is connected to His saying: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ, and the lam (ل) is for an oath.

His saying, the Exalted: فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَثابَهُمْ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً وَمَغانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَها وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً — He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down composure on them, and He rewarded them with a victory near at hand, and abundant spoils that they will take; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise (48:18-19). This is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ etc. What is meant by "what was in their hearts" is the goodness and sincerity of intention in their pledge of allegiance, for an action is only pleasing to Allah not by its outward form and appearance, but by the truthfulness and sincerity of the intention (النية).

The meaning is: He knew what was in their hearts of truthful intention and sincerity in their pledging allegiance to you.

It has been said: What is meant by "what was in their hearts" is faith (الإيمان), its soundness, love for the religion, and eagerness for it. It has also been said: It refers to the distress and pride (الأنفة) regarding the leniency shown to the polytheists and the peace treaty with them. However, the context (السياق) does not support either of these two views, as is not hidden.

If you say: What is meant by "what was in their hearts" is not absolutely everything in them, but rather their truthful and sincere intention in the pledge of allegiance as mentioned. His knowledge, the Exalted, of their intention characterized by truthfulness and sincerity is a cause (سبب) from which His pleasure with them is derived, not a result (مسبب) derived from the pleasure. The implication of this would be the derivation of pleasure from knowledge, such that it would be said: "He certainly knew what was in their hearts, so He was pleased with them," not the derivation of knowledge from pleasure as in the verse.

I say: Just as a result (المسبب) is derived from a cause (السبب) in terms of actualization and existence, so too is the cause—whether complete or incomplete—derived from the result in terms of disclosure and manifestation. Pleasure (الرضا), as previously mentioned, is an attribute of action for Him, the Exalted, abstracted from the totality of His knowledge of the righteous deed and that which He rewards and recompenses the doer with. That from which pleasure is abstracted in this instance is the totality of His knowledge of what was in their hearts, His sending down tranquility upon them, and His rewarding them with a near victory and abundant spoils they would take.

Thus, His saying: فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ etc., is a derivation from His saying: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ to indicate the reality of this pleasure and to disclose the totality of matters through whose actualization the meaning of pleasure is realized.

Then, His saying: فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ is derived from His saying: فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ, as is what is coordinated to it in His saying: وَأَثابَهُمْ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً etc.

What is meant by the "near victory" (الفتح القريب) is the conquest of Khaybar, as indicated by the context, and likewise, what is meant by "abundant spoils that they will take" are the spoils of Khaybar. It has been said: What is meant by the near victory is the conquest of Mecca, but the context does not support this.

And His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً means He is predominant in what He wills, precise in His action, and never arbitrary in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَدَكُمُ اللهُ مَغانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَها فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هذِهِ etc. — Allah has promised you many spoils that you will capture. So He has expedited this for you (48:20). What is meant by these "many spoils" are the spoils that the believers would take after returning from al-Hudaybiyyah, encompassing the spoils of Khaybar and others. Thus, the indication in His saying: فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هذِهِ (So He has expedited this for you) refers to the spoils mentioned in the previous verse, which are the spoils of Khaybar; they were treated as present due to the proximity of their occurrence.

This is on the assumption that the verse was revealed along with the preceding verses. As for what has been said—that the verse was revealed after the conquest of Khaybar—then the matter of the indication in His saying: فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هذِهِ is obvious. However, it is well-known that the Surah was revealed in its entirety during the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return from al-Hudaybiyyah, between it and Medina.

It has also been said: The indication in "this" (هذه) refers to the pledge of allegiance they made under the tree, but this is as you see [unlikely].

And His saying: وَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَ النَّاسِ عَنْكُمْ — and He has held back the hands of men from you (48:20). It has been said: What is meant by "the people" (الناس) are the tribes of Asad and Ghatafan; they intended, after the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) march to Khaybar, to raid the wealth and families of the Muslims in Medina, but Allah cast terror into their hearts and stayed their hands.

It has also been said: What is meant is Malik ibn ‘Awf and ‘Uyaynah ibn Hisn along with the tribes of Asad and Ghatafan; they came to aid the Jews of Khaybar, but Allah cast terror into their hearts and they returned. It has also been said: What is meant by "the people" are the people of Mecca and their allies, as they did not fight him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and agreed to the peace treaty.

And His saying: وَلِتَكُونَ آيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — and that it may be a sign for the believers (48:20). This is coordinated to a suppressed phrase, meaning: Allah promised them this reward—the reward of victory and the abundant spoils, both expedited and deferred—for such and such interests, and "that it may be a sign (آية) for the believers," i.e., a mark and an indicator that guides them to the fact that they are upon the truth, that their Lord is truthful in His promise, and that their Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is truthful in his reports.

The Surah has included several reports of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — in which there is guidance for the God-conscious (المتقين), such as His saying: سَيَقُولُ لَكَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ شَغَلَتْنا etc., and His saying: سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انْطَلَقْتُمْ etc., and His saying: قُلُوبُ لِلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ etc., and what is in these verses of the promise of victory and spoils, and His saying later: وَأُخْرى لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْها etc., and His saying later: لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيا etc.

And His saying: وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً — and that He may guide you to a straight path (48:20). This is coordinated to "that it may be" (لتكون), meaning: and that He may guide you to a straight path (صراطاً مستقيماً) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — which is the path leading to the exaltation of the word of truth and the spreading of the religion. It has been said: It is trust in Allah and reliance (التوكل) upon Him in all that you do and leave, but what we have mentioned is more in accord with the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُخْرى لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْها قَدْ أَحاطَ اللهُ بِها وَكانَ اللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيراً — And other [spoils as well] which you have not yet captured: Allah has already encompassed them, and Allah has power over all things (48:21). Meaning: and other spoils which you were not able to capture, Allah has already encompassed them with an encompassment of power, and Allah has power over all things.

Thus, His saying: أُخْرى (other) is a subject (مبتدأ), and لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْها is its attribute, and His saying: قَدْ أَحاطَ اللهُ بِها is its second predicate (خبر), while its first predicate is suppressed. The estimation of the speech is: "And there are other spoils which Allah has encompassed."

It has been said: His saying أُخْرى is in the accusative case (نصب) by coordination to His saying هذِهِ, the estimation being: "and He expedited for you other spoils." It has also been said: it is in the accusative by a suppressed verb, the estimation being: "and He decreed other spoils." It has also been said: it is in the genitive case (جر) by the estimation of rubba (رب), the estimation being: "and many other spoils." These are views, none of which is free from weakness.

What is meant by "the other" in the verse—according to what has been said—are the spoils of Hawazin. It has also been said: what is meant are the spoils of Persia and Rome. It has also been said: what is meant is the conquest of Mecca, and the described noun is suppressed, the estimation being: "and another town (قرية) you were not able to capture," i.e., its conquest. The first of these views is the closest.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ قاتَلَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوَلَّوُا الْأَدْبارَ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً — If the faithless fight you, they will surely turn their backs [to flee], then they will find neither any guardian nor helper (48:22). This is another report in which Allah, the Glorified, informs them of the weakness of the disbelievers in fighting the believers by themselves, and that they have no guardian (ولي) to take charge of their affairs nor any helper (نصير) to assist them. It concludes that they are not strong enough in themselves to fight you, nor do they have any helper from the Bedouins to assist them. This in itself is a glad tiding for the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: سُنَّةَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللهِ تَبْدِيلاً — [This is] Allah’s precedent which has passed before, and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent (48:23). "Allah's precedent" (سُنَّةَ اللهِ) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for a suppressed verb, i.e., He has established the precedent of Allah. This is an ancient precedent of His, the Glorified: that He makes His prophets and the believers in them prevail over their enemies among those who disbelieve, provided they are truthful in their faith and sincere in their intentions. وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللهِ تَبْدِيلاً (and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent), just as the Exalted said: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي — Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’ (58:21). The Muslims were never afflicted in any of their battles except due to some opposition they showed toward Allah and His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنْكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ etc. — It is He who held back their hands from you, and your hands from them, in the valley of Meccca, after He had given you victory over them (48:24). It appears that what is meant by holding back the hands of each group from the other is what occurred of the peace treaty between the two parties at al-Hudaybiyyah, which is "the valley of Mecca" (بطن مكة) due to its proximity to it and its connection to it, such that it is said some of its lands are from the Sanctuary (الحرم). This is because each of the two groups was the bitterest enemy of the other; Quraysh had taken care to gather a large force from themselves and the Abyssinians, and the believers had pledged allegiance to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to fight, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had resolved to engage the people. Allah had given the Prophet and those who believed victory over the disbelievers when they entered their land and established their footing in the heart of their territory, such that nothing could be imagined between them except fighting. However, Allah, the Glorified, held back the hands of the disbelievers from the believers and the hands of the believers from the disbelievers after the believers had gained victory over them; and Allah is watchful of what they do.

His saying, the Exalted: هُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَالْهَدْيَ مَعْكُوفاً أَنْ يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُ — They are the ones who disbelieved and barred you from the Sacred Mosque, and [barred] the sacrificial offering from reaching its destination (48:25). Devotion (العكوف) to a matter is to remain constant upon it. The "restrained" (المعكوف)—as in al-Majma‘—is that which is prevented from going in a direction by being kept in its place; from this comes i‘tikaf (الاعتكاف), which is staying in the mosque for worship.

The meaning is: The polytheists—the polytheists of Mecca—are those who disbelieved and prevented you from the Sacred Mosque, and prevented the sacrificial offering (الهدي)—which you had brought—while it was restrained from reaching its destination (محله), i.e., the place where it is to be sacrificed or slaughtered, which is Mecca where the offering of the ‘umrah is sacrificed, just as the offering of the hajj is sacrificed in Mina. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those believers with him were in the state of ihram for ‘umrah and had brought sacrificial offerings for that purpose.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا رِجالٌ مُؤْمِنُونَ وَنِساءٌ مُؤْمِناتٌ لَمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَنْ تَطَؤُهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُمْ مِنْهُمْ مَعَرَّةٌ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ — And were it not for [certain] believing men and believing women whom you did not know—lest you should trample them, and thus the blame for [killing] them should fall on you unknowingly (48:25). Trampling (الوطء) is treading upon, and harm (المعرة) is something disliked. His saying: أَنْ تَطَؤُهُمْ is an appositive of inclusion (بدل اشتمال) for the subject of lawla (لو لا), and the apodosis (جواب) of lawla is suppressed, the estimation being: "He would not have held back your hands from them."

The meaning is: Were it not for the fact that you might trample believing men and believing women in Mecca while you were ignorant of them, not knowing them, and thus something disliked would afflict you from their killing and destruction, Allah would not have held back your hands from them.

And His saying: لِيُدْخِلَ اللهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — so that Allah may admit into His mercy whomever He wishes (48:25). The lam (ل) is connected to a suppressed phrase, the estimation being: "But He held back your hands from them so that He may admit into His mercy those non-distinct believers and believing women by their safety from being killed, and you by protecting you from the affliction of harm."

It has been said: The meaning is: so that He may admit into His mercy those of the disbelievers who would embrace Islam after the peace treaty.

And His saying: لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — Had they been distinct, We would have surely punished the faithless among them with a painful punishment (48:25). Separation (التزيل) is dispersing. The pronoun in "they were distinct" (تزيلوا) refers to all those previously mentioned—the believers and the disbelievers among the people of Mecca. Meaning: if they had been separated such that the believers were distinguished from the disbelievers, We would have surely punished the disbelievers among the people of Mecca with a painful punishment, but We did not punish them out of respect for the believers who were mixed among them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ — When the faithless harboured in their hearts a disdain, the disdain of [the days of] pagan ignorance (48:26) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "The irascible power (القوة الغضبية) when it is stirred and increases is expressed as hamiyyah (الحمية); it is said: 'I became hot (حميت) against so-and-so,' meaning I became angry with him. The Exalted said: حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ. From this is borrowed their saying: 'I protected (حميت) the place as a sanctuary (حمى).'" End quote.

The adverbial phrase in His saying: إِذْ جَعَلَ is connected to His previous saying: وَصَدُّوكُمْ. It has been said: it is connected to His saying: لَعَذَّبْنَا. It has also been said: it is connected to a suppressed "remember" (اذكر). "Making" (الجعل) is in the sense of casting; "those who disbelieved" is its subject, and "disdain" (الحمية) is its object. "The disdain of pagan ignorance" (حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ) is an explanation for the disdain, and "ignorance" (الجاهلية) is an attribute placed in the position of the described noun, the estimation being "the ignorant creed" (الملة الجاهلية).

If "making" (جعل) were in the sense of "transforming" (صير), its second object would be suppressed, the estimation being: "when those who disbelieved made disdain firmly rooted in their hearts." The explicit noun was placed in the position of the pronoun in His saying: جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا to indicate the cause of the judgment.

The meaning of the verse is: They are those who disbelieved and barred you when they cast into their hearts disdain, the disdain of the ignorant creed.

And His saying: فأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — then Allah sent down His composure on His Apostle and on the believers (48:26). This is a derivation from His saying: جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا and it provides a type of contrast, as if it were said: they placed disdain in their hearts, so Allah, the Glorified, countered it by sending down tranquility upon His Messenger and upon the believers, so their hearts became tranquil and they were not swayed by recklessness; they displayed composure and dignity without being provoked by ignorance.

And His saying: وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى — and made them abide by the word of God-consciousness (48:26). Meaning: He made it stay with them, never to be separated from them. It is—according to what the majority of commentators have chosen—the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. It has been said: what is meant is steadfastness upon the covenant and fulfillment of it. It has also been said: it means tranquility (السكينة). It has also been said: it refers to their saying "Yes" (بلى) in the world of pre-existence (عالم الذر), which is the weakest of opinions.

It is not unlikely that what is meant by it is the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان) which commands godwariness, as the Exalted said: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرروحٍ مِنْهُ — He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him (58:22). Allah has applied the term "word" (الكلمة) to the spirit in His saying: وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ — and His Word that He cast toward Mary, and a spirit from Him (4:171).

And His saying: وَكانُوا أَحَقَّ بِها وَأَهْلَها — and they were worthier of it and deserved it (48:26). As for their being worthier of it, it is due to the completeness of their readiness to receive this divine gift because of the righteous deeds they performed; thus, they were worthier of it than others. As for their being its people (أهلها), it is because they are specifically characterized by it and it is not found in others, and the "people" of a thing are those specifically associated with it.

It has been said: what is meant is that they were worthier of tranquility and its people. It has also been said: there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech, the original being: "and they were its people and worthier of it," but this is as you see [unlikely].

And His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيماً — and Allah has knowledge of all things (48:26). This is a concluding remark (تذييل) for His saying: وَكانُوا أَحَقَّ بِها وَأَهْلَها or for all that preceded. The meaning according to both views is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيا بِالْحَقِّ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ آمِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُؤُسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لا تَخافُونَ etc. — Certainly Allah has fulfilled His Apostle’s vision in all truth: You will surely enter the Sacred Mosque, if Allah wills, in safety, with your heads shaven or hair cut short, and without any fear (48:27). It has been said: sadaqa (صدق) and kadhaba (كذب) in their light form take two objects; it is said: "I told Zayd the truth in the account" and "I told him a lie in the account." They can also take the second object with the preposition fi (في). In their doubled form (saddaqa and kadhaba), they take one object.

The lam (ل) in لَقَدْ صَدَقَ اللهُ is for an oath, and His saying: لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ is the response to the oath.

His saying: بِالْحَقِ is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the vision, and the ba (ب) in it is for accompaniment. The suspension upon the will in His saying: إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ (if Allah wills) is for the instruction of the servants. The meaning is: I swear, Allah has certainly fulfilled for His Messenger the vision He showed him; you shall surely enter, O believers, the Sacred Mosque, if Allah wills, while you are secure from the evil of the polytheists, with your heads shaved or hair cut short, not fearing the polytheists.

And His saying: فَعَلِمَ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا فَجَعَلَ مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ فَتْحاً قَرِيباً — He knew what you did not know, and He has appointed for you besides that a victory near at hand (48:27). "That" (ذلك) is an indication to what preceded of their entering the Sacred Mosque in safety. What is meant by His saying: مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ is "sooner than that." The meaning is: He, the Exalted, knew of the benefit in your entering the Sacred Mosque in safety what you were ignorant of and did not know; therefore, He appointed before your entering in that manner a near victory so that entering in that manner would be facilitated for you.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by the "near victory" in this verse is the victory of al-Hudaybiyyah, for it is what paved the way for the believers to enter the Sacred Mosque in safety and facilitated that for them. Were it not for that, it would not have been possible for them to enter it except through fighting and bloodshed, and there would have been no ‘umrah with that. However, the peace treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah and the conditions stipulated therein enabled them to enter the Mosque as pilgrims in the following year.

From this you know that the saying of some—that what is meant by the near victory in the verse is the conquest of Khaybar—is far from the context. As for the saying that it is the conquest of Mecca, it is even further.

The context of the verse suggests that what is meant by it is the removal of doubt from some of those who were with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). For the believers assumed from a vision seen by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of their entering the Mosque in safety, with their heads shaved and hair cut short, that they would enter it in that manner in that very year. When they went out intending Mecca for ‘umrah and the polytheists intercepted them at al-Hudaybiyyah and barred them from the Sacred Mosque, some of them doubted the vision. So Allah removed their doubt with what is in the verse.

The summary of it is: The vision is true, shown by Allah to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and He, the Exalted, has fulfilled that; you will enter the Sacred Mosque, if Allah wills, in safety, with your heads shaved and hair cut short, without fear. However, He, the Exalted, delayed it and preceded it with this victory—which is the peace treaty of al-Hudaybiyyah—so that your entry might be facilitated, due to His knowledge, the Exalted, that it would not be possible for you to enter it in safety, with your heads shaved and hair cut short, without fear, except through this path.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ etc. — It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the religion of truth, that He may make it prevail over all religions (48:28). Its interpretation has already been given in Surah al-Tawbah, verse 33. And His saying: وَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً — and Allah suffices as a witness (48:28), i.e., as a witness to the truth of his prophethood and the promise that his religion will prevail over all religions, or that his vision is true. Thus, the sentence is a concluding remark looking toward the verse itself or the preceding verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ the verse: Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Salamah ibn al-Akwa‘, who said: "While we were taking a midday nap, the caller of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called out: 'O people! The pledge! The pledge! The Holy Spirit has descended!' So we rushed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and he was under a gum acacia tree—and we pledged allegiance to him. That is the saying of Allah, the Exalted: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ. He pledged for ‘Uthman with one of his hands upon the other. The people said: 'Congratulations to the son of ‘Affan; he is circumambulating the House while we are here.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Even if he were to stay for such and such years, he would not circumambulate until I circumambulate.'"

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Muslim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ma‘qil ibn Yasar, who said: "I saw myself on the Day of the Tree while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was taking the pledge from the people—and I was lifting a branch of its branches from his head—and we were fourteen hundred. We did not pledge allegiance to him upon death, but we pledged to him that we would not flee."

I say: The fact that the believers on that day were fourteen hundred is narrated in other reports. In some reports, they were thirteen hundred, and in some up to eighteen hundred. Likewise, the fact that the pledge was not to flee is in some reports, while in others it was upon death.

In the same source, Ahmad narrated from Jabir, and Muslim from Umm Bishr from him, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "None of those who pledged allegiance under the tree shall enter the Fire."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ, he said: "Tranquility was only sent down upon those from whom fulfillment (الوفاء) was known."

I say: This narration specifies what preceded it, and it is supported by His saying, the Exalted, previously: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ يَدُ اللهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَوْفى بِما عاهَدَ عَلَيْهُ اللهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً — Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands. Then whosoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own soul’s detriment, and whosoever fulfils the covenant he has made with Allah, He will give him a great reward (48:10). Thus, He stipulated fulfillment and the absence of breaking the oath for the reward—and this necessitates the same stipulation for pleasure. Al-Qummi mentioned this meaning in his Tafsir, and it appears to be a narration.

In al-Durr al-Manthur as well: regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا the verse: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il narrated from Sahl ibn Hunayf that he said on the Day of Siffin: "Suspect your own opinions! For I saw us on the Day of al-Hudaybiyyah deferring to the peace treaty—which was between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the polytheists—and if we had seen a way to fight, we would have fought."

‘Umar came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Are we not upon the truth and they upon falsehood?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Are not our slain in Paradise and their slain in the Fire?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Then why should we give the low hand in our religion? And return before Allah has judged between us and them?" He said: "O son of al-Khattab! I am the Messenger of Allah, and Allah will never forsake me."

He returned in a state of anger and could not remain patient until he came to Abu Bakr and said: "O Abu Bakr! Are we not upon the truth and they upon falsehood?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Are not our slain in Paradise and their slain in the Fire?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Then why should we give the low hand in our religion?" He said: "O son of al-Khattab! He is the Messenger of Allah, and Allah will never forsake him." Then Surah al-Fath was revealed, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent for ‘Umar and recited it to him. He said: "O Messenger of Allah! Is it a victory?" He said: "Yes."

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain of narration from Mansur ibn Hazim from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً, he said: "If Allah had brought out what was in the loins of the believers of the disbelievers—and what was in the loins of the disbelievers of the believers—We would have surely punished those who disbelieved."

I say: This meaning is narrated in other reports.

And with its chain of narration from Jamil, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about His saying, the Exalted: وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى, and he said: 'It is faith (الإيمان).'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tirmidhi, ‘Abdullah ibn Ahmad in Zawa’id al-Musnad, Ibn Jarir, al-Daraqutni in al-Ifrad, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding: وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى, he said: "It is: There is no god but Allah (لا إله إلا الله)."

I say: This meaning is also narrated through other routes from ‘Ali, Salamah ibn al-Akwa‘, and Abu Hurayrah. It is also narrated through Shi‘i routes as in al-‘Ilal, with its chain of narration from al-Hasan ibn ‘Abdillah from his fathers from his grandfather al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): in a hadith in which he interprets "Glory be to Allah, and praise be to Allah, and there is no god but Allah, and Allah is the Greatest," he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "And His saying: 'There is no god but Allah' means His oneness (وحدانيته)—Allah does not accept deeds except with it, and it is the word of godwariness (كلمة التقوى)—Allah makes the scales heavy with it on the Day of Resurrection."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the story of the conquest of Khaybar, it says: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived in Medina from al-Hudaybiyyah, he stayed there for twenty nights, then he went out early to Khaybar."

Ibn Ishaq mentioned with his chain of narration to Abu Marwan al-Aslami from his father from his grandfather, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Khaybar until we were near it and overlooked it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Stop!' and the people stopped. Then he said: 'O Allah, Lord of the seven heavens and what they overshadow, and Lord of the seven earths and what they carry, and Lord of the devils and those they lead astray! We ask You for the good of this village, the good of its people, and the good of what is in it; and we seek refuge in You from the evil of this village, the evil of its people, and the evil of what is in it. Proceed in the Name of Allah!'"

From Salamah ibn al-Akwa‘, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Khaybar and traveled by night. A man from the people said to ‘Amir ibn al-Akwa‘: 'Will you not let us hear some of your verses?' ‘Amir was a poet, so he began saying:

O Allah! Were it not for You, we would not have been guided, Nor given charity, nor prayed. So forgive—may we be a ransom for You—what we have acquired, And make our feet firm if we meet [the enemy], And send down tranquility upon us. Indeed, when we are called out to, we come, And with the cry, they have relied upon us.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Who is this driver?' They said: '‘Amir.' He said: 'May Allah have mercy on him.' ‘Umar, who was on a camel of his that was exhausted, said: 'O Messenger of Allah! If only you had let us enjoy him [longer],' and that was because the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never asked for forgiveness for a man specifically except that he was martyred."

They said: "When the war intensified and the people stood in ranks, a Jew came out saying:

Khaybar has known that I am Marhab, Fully armed, a tested hero, When wars approach, blazing.

‘Amir went out to him saying:

Khaybar has known that I am ‘Amir, Fully armed, a daring hero.

They exchanged two blows; the Jew's sword fell upon ‘Amir's shield. ‘Amir's sword was short, so he reached for the Jew's leg to strike him, but the tip of his sword returned and struck the middle of ‘Amir's knee, and he died from it."

Salamah said: "Some of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were saying: '‘Amir's deed has been nullified; he killed himself.' I came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while I was crying and said: 'They say that ‘Amir's deed has been nullified.' He said: 'Who said that?' I said: 'A group of your companions.' He said: 'They have lied; rather, he shall be given his reward twice.'"

He said: "We besieged them until we were afflicted by severe hunger. Then Allah opened it for us. That was because the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) gave the banner to ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, and those who were with him from the people rose. They met the people of Khaybar, but ‘Umar and his companions were exposed and retreated to the Messenger of Allah, he accusing them of cowardice and they accusing him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had been afflicted by a migraine and had not come out to the people. When he recovered from his pain, he said: 'What have the people done at Khaybar?' He was informed, so he said: 'Tomorrow I shall surely give the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and whom Allah and His Messenger love; a constant attacker, never a fleer, who will not return until Allah grants victory at his hands.'"

Al-Bukhari and Muslim narrated from Qutaybah ibn Sa‘id, who said: Ya‘qub narrated to us from ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Iskandarani from Abu Hazim, who said: Sahl ibn Sa‘d informed me that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said on the day of Khaybar: "Tomorrow I shall surely give this banner to a man at whose hands Allah will grant victory; he loves Allah and His Messenger, and Allah and His Messenger love him."

He said: "The people spent the night talking among themselves as to which of them would be given it. When morning came, the people went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), all of them hoping to be given it."

He said: "Where is ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib?" They said: "O Messenger of Allah! He is complaining of his eyes." He said: "Send for him." So he was brought, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) spat in his eyes, and he was healed as if he had no pain. He gave him the banner. ‘Ali said: "O Messenger of Allah! Shall I fight them until they are like us?" He said: "Proceed with composure until you descend into their courtyard, then invite them to Islam and inform them of what is obligatory upon them of Allah's right. For by Allah, that Allah should guide one man through you is better for you than having red camels."

Salamah said: "Marhab came out saying: 'Khaybar has known that I am Marhab...' the verses. ‘Ali came out to him saying:

I am the one whom my mother named Haydarah, Like a lion of the forests, grim of appearance, I give them their full measure with a swift stroke.

He struck Marhab, splitting his head and killing him, and the victory was at his hands."

Muslim recorded it in his Sahih.

Abu ‘Abdillah al-Hafiz narrated with his chain of narration from Abu Rafi‘, the freed slave of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "We went out with ‘Ali when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent him. When he drew near the fortress, its people came out to him and he fought them. A man from the Jews struck him and knocked his shield from his hand. ‘Ali reached for the gate of the fortress and used it as a shield for himself. It remained in his hand while he fought until Allah granted him victory. Then he cast it from his hand. I saw myself among a group of seven, I being the eighth, struggling to turn that gate over, but we were unable to turn it."

With his chain of narration from Layth ibn Abi Sulaym from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali, who said: Jabir ibn ‘Abdillah narrated to me that ‘Ali carried the gate on the day of Khaybar until the Muslims climbed upon it and stormed it, and that it was moved afterward and forty men could not carry it.

He said: It is narrated from another route from Jabir: "Then seventy men gathered against it, and their utmost effort was only to return the gate [to its place]."

With his chain of narration from ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Layla, who said: "‘Ali used to wear a thick, stuffed cloak in both the heat and the winter and did not mind the heat. My companions came to me and said: 'We have seen something from the Commander of the Faithful; have you seen it?' I said: 'What is it?' They said: 'We see him coming out to us in the intense heat in a thick, stuffed cloak and he does not mind the heat, and he comes out to us in the intense cold in two light garments and he does not mind the cold. Have you heard anything regarding that?' I said: 'No.' They said: 'Then ask your father about that for us, for he spends the evening in conversation with him.' So I asked him, and he said: 'I have not heard anything regarding that.'"

He entered upon ‘Ali and spent the evening in conversation with him, then asked him about that. He said: "Did you not witness Khaybar?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Did you not see the Messenger of Allah when he called Abu Bakr, tied the banner for him, and sent him to the people? He went and met the people, then came back with the people having been defeated. Then he sent for ‘Umar, tied the banner for him, and sent him to the people. He went and met the people, fought them, then returned having been defeated."

"The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Today I shall surely give the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and whom Allah and His Messenger love; Allah will grant victory at his hands; a constant attacker, never a fleer.' He called me and gave me the banner, then said: 'O Allah! Protect him from heat and cold.' I never felt heat or cold after that." All of this is quoted from the book Dala’il al-Nubuwwah by Imam Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi.

Al-Tabarsi said: "Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) continued to conquer the fortresses one by one and take possession of the wealth until they reached the fortress of al-Watih and al-Salalim, which were the last of the fortresses of Khaybar to be conquered. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) besieged them for over ten nights."

Ibn Ishaq said: "When al-Qamus, the fortress of Abu al-Huqayq, was conquered, Safiyyah bint Huyayy ibn Akhtab was brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) along with another woman. Bilal passed with them—he being the one who brought them—by some of the slain Jews. When the one with Safiyyah saw them, she cried out, struck her face, and poured dust on her head. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw her, he said: 'Take this she-devil away from me!' He ordered Safiyyah to be placed behind him and cast his cloak over her, so the Muslims knew that he had chosen her for himself. He said to Bilal when he saw what he saw from that Jewish woman: 'Has mercy been stripped from you, O Bilal, that you pass with two women by the slain bodies of their men?' Safiyyah had seen in a dream—while she was a bride to Kinanah ibn al-Rabi‘ ibn Abi al-Huqayq—that a moon fell into her lap. She recounted her vision to her husband, and he said: 'This is nothing but that you desire the King of the Hijaz, Muhammad.' He slapped her face with a slap that turned her eye green. She was brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the mark of it upon her. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asked her what it was, and she informed him."

Ibn Abi al-Huqayq sent to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Shall I come down and speak to you?" He said: "Yes." So he came down and made peace with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the condition of sparing the blood of the combatants in their fortresses and leaving the children to them. They would leave Khaybar and its land with their children and leave between the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and what they possessed of wealth, land, gold, silver, horses, and furniture, and all cloth except a garment on a person's back. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Then the protection of Allah and the protection of His Messenger are removed from you if you hide anything from me." So they made peace with him on that.

When the people of Fadak heard what they had done, they sent to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asking him to let them go, spare their blood, and they would leave between him and the wealth. He did so. Among those who mediated between the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and them in that was Muhayyisah ibn Mas‘ud, one of the Banu Harithah.

When the people of Khaybar surrendered on those terms, they asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to let them work the wealth for half [the produce], saying: "We are more knowledgeable of it than you and better at cultivating it." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) made peace with them for half, on the condition that: "If we wish to expel you, we shall expel you." The people of Fadak made peace with him on similar terms. Thus, the wealth of Khaybar was spoils (فيئاً) among the Muslims, while Fadak was exclusively for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) because they did not urge on horses or camels against it.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was settled, Zaynab bint al-Harith—the wife of Sallam ibn Mishkam and the niece of Marhab—gifted him a roasted sheep. She had asked which part was most beloved to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and it was said to her: "The shoulder." So she put much poison in it and poisoned the rest of the sheep, then brought it. When she placed it before him, he took the shoulder, took a morsel from it and bit into it. With him was Bishr ibn al-Bara’ ibn Ma‘rur, who took a bone and bit into it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Lift your hands! For the shoulder of this sheep informs me that it is poisoned." Then he called her and she confessed. He said: "What prompted you to do that?" She said: "You have done to my people what is not hidden from you. I said: 'If he is a prophet, he will be informed; and if he is a king, I will be relieved of him.'" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) pardoned her, and Bishr ibn al-Bara’ died from the bite he had eaten.

He said: The mother of Bishr ibn al-Bara’ entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to visit him in his illness in which he passed away. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O mother of Bishr! The morsel of Khaybar which I ate at Khaybar with your son has not ceased to return to me; this is the time it has severed my aorta." The Muslims used to consider that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) died as a martyr, along with what Allah had honored him with of prophethood.

Ayah

مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ أَشِدَّآءُ عَلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَآءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَىٰهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِى وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلسُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلْإِنجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْـَٔهُۥ فَـَٔازَرَهُۥ فَٱسْتَغْلَظَ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَىٰ سُوقِهِۦ يُعْجِبُ ٱلزُّرَّاعَ لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ ۗ وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًۢا (٢٩)
Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace, and [His] pleasure. Their mark is [visible] on their faces, from the effect of prostration. Such is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel. Like a tillage that sends out its shoots and builds them up, and they grow stout and settle on their stalks, impressing the sowers, so that He may enrage the faithless by them. Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (29)

(Commentary)

This verse is the conclusion of the Surah. It describes the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those with him using the same descriptions found in the Torah and the Gospel, and it promises those among them who believe and perform righteous deeds a beautiful promise. The verse is connected to what preceded it, where He informed that He sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth.

His saying, the Exalted: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ — Muhammad is the Apostle of Allah (48:29) until the end of the verse. It is apparent that this is a subject and a predicate, forming a complete sentence. It has also been said that مُحَمَّدٌ (Muhammad) is the predicate of an omitted subject, which is a pronoun referring back to "the Messenger" in the previous verse, the implied meaning being: "He is Muhammad." In this case, رَسُولُ اللهِ (the Apostle of Allah) would be an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان), an adjective (صفة), or a substitute (بدل). It has also been suggested that مُحَمَّدٌ is the subject, while رَسُولُ اللهِ is an explanatory apposition, an adjective, or a substitute, and الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ (those who are with him) is conjoined to the subject, with أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ (hard against the faithless) etc., being the predicate of the subject.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ — and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves (48:29) is a subject and a predicate. The speech is directed toward describing those who are with him, and the mentioned hardness and mercy are among their attributes.

The following of the phrase أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ (hard against the faithless) with the phrase رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ (merciful amongst themselves) is to dispel any potential illusion that their being hard against the disbelievers might necessitate some hardness among themselves. Thus, He dispelled this with His saying: رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ (merciful amongst themselves). The two sentences together indicate that their conduct toward the disbelievers is one of hardness, while their conduct among the believers is one of mercy.

His saying: تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً — You see them bowing and prostrating (48:29). The terms الركع (those bowing) and السجد (those prostrating) are the plurals of راكع (one who bows) and ساجد (one who prostrates). What is meant by their being "bowing and prostrating" is their performance of prayer (الصلاة). The verb تَراهُمْ (you see them) implies continuity (الاستمرار).
